zones.name: North Lake Tahoe
zones.description: The quieter, outdoor-recreation-focused side of Lake Tahoe encompassing Truckee, Tahoe City, Kings Beach, and Incline Village. Features extensive trail networks, ski resorts (Palisades Tahoe, Northstar), and access to the Tahoe Rim Trail.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP: Strong multi-activity hub with hiking, running, walking, and bouldering options. Distinct from South Lake Tahoe in character — more wilderness-oriented. One of the most visited outdoor recreation destinations in California. Sources: NPS, Tahoe National Forest, Visit Lake Tahoe.

zones.name: South Lake Tahoe
zones.description: The larger, entertainment-and-recreation resort side of Lake Tahoe, featuring Heavenly ski resort, Emerald Bay State Park, Desolation Wilderness, and numerous beaches. Distinct from North Tahoe in visitor demographics and amenity profile.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP: Distinct destination with massive annual visitation (3M+). Strong outdoor recreation including hiking in Desolation Wilderness, trail running, and some bouldering. Justifies its own zone separate from North Lake Tahoe. Sources: California State Parks, El Dorado National Forest.

zones.name: Tuolumne Meadows
zones.description: High-alpine subalpine meadow at 8,619 ft in Yosemite National Park, accessed via Tioga Road (Highway 120). Distinct from Yosemite Valley — more remote, less crowded, with access to PCT/JMT, Cathedral Lakes, and extensive high-country hiking. Summer-only access (road closes Nov–May).
zones.validation_notes: ADDED: Significant tourism destination distinct from Yosemite Valley in character, elevation, and activity profile. World-class hiking, some of the best high-altitude trail running in California, and high granite domes with bouldering/scrambling. 600,000+ annual visitors. Sources: NPS Yosemite.

zones.name: Mammoth Lakes
zones.description: Major Eastern Sierra resort town at 7,800 ft with year-round tourism. Home to Mammoth Mountain ski area, Devils Postpile National Monument, Inyo National Forest trail networks, June Mountain, and proximity to the JMT southern terminus area.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP: One of California's top outdoor recreation destinations. Strong multi-activity profile — hiking (Mammoth Lakes Basin, Duck Pass), trail running (Mammoth Trail Fest), walking (bike path network), and bouldering (Mammoth Rock area). Sources: Inyo National Forest, Town of Mammoth Lakes.

zones.name: Bishop/Eastern Sierra
zones.description: Gateway city to the Eastern Sierra at the foot of the White Mountains, most famous for world-class bouldering at the Buttermilks and Happy Boulders. Also offers premier high-altitude hiking (Bishop Pass, Pine Creek), multi-day JMT access, and year-round trail running.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP: World-class bouldering destination (Buttermilks featured in major climbing competitions). Premier zone for all four activity types. Bishop Creek Canyon offers exceptional hiking and walking. Trail running is huge here due to 300+ sunny days. Sources: Bishop Visitor Center, Inyo National Forest, Mountain Project.

zones.name: Yosemite Valley
zones.description: Iconic 7-mile glacially carved valley at 3,970 ft in Yosemite National Park, featuring El Capitan, Half Dome, Bridalveil Fall, and Yosemite Falls. World-famous hiking, world-class big wall climbing/bouldering (Camp 4), and family-friendly valley walks. 4M+ annual visitors.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP: Most visited national park unit in the Sierra Nevada. Justifies its own zone — distinct from Tuolumne Meadows in elevation, accessibility, and crowd profile. World-class bouldering at Camp 4 area. Sources: NPS Yosemite.

zones.name: Sequoia/Kings Canyon
zones.description: Twin national parks encompassing the Giant Forest (world's largest trees by volume), Kings Canyon (North America's deepest canyon), Cedar Grove, and General Grant Grove. Strong hiking, walking, and family recreation. Managed jointly by NPS but distinct in character.
zones.validation_notes: KEEP (combined): While Sequoia and Kings Canyon are distinct parks, they share an entrance fee, adjoining road network, and are commonly visited together. Combined zone avoids artificial fragmentation for this dataset's purposes. Strong activity support — hiking, family walks around groves, some trail running. Note: Placerville/El Dorado Foothills was REMOVED — it is Gold Country foothills geography, not Sierra Nevada proper, and lacks the multi-activity mountain recreation profile needed for this dataset. Sources: NPS Sequoia/Kings Canyon.

places.name: South Lake Shoreline Walk

places.parking_notes: USFS paved lot at South Lake (9,755 ft). Fee required. Restrooms available. Lot fills early on summer weekends; arrive before 8 AM. America the Beautiful pass accepted.

places.name: Bishop Creek Trail (Lower Canyon Walk)
places.address: West Line Street / Bishop Creek, Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Street parking along West Line Street near the creek. No fee. Public access trail along Bishop Creek through the south edge of town.

places.name: Rock Creek Lake Shore Walk
places.address: Rock Creek Lake, Rock Creek Road, Tom's Place, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: USFS day-use parking at Rock Creek Lake (10,850 ft). Small fee. Restrooms available at Rock Creek Lake Resort. Road open typically June–October.

places.name: Bishop City Park Loop
places.address: 501 W. China Lake Blvd (Hwy 168 W), Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Free paved parking lot inside Bishop City Park. Large lot, rarely fills. Restrooms and picnic tables on site.

places.name: Lake Sabrina Picnic Area Family Walk
places.address: Lake Sabrina Road, Bishop Creek Canyon, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: USFS paved lot at Lake Sabrina. Fee required. Vault toilets. No stroller-friendly path beyond picnic area; carry infants on trail.

places.name: North Lake to Lamarck Col Trailhead Area Walk
places.address: North Lake Road (off Lake Sabrina Road), Bishop Creek Canyon, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Small USFS dirt parking area at North Lake (9,440 ft). Limited to ~20 vehicles. Arrive early (before 7 AM) on summer weekends. Vault toilet. No fee for day use on this spur.

places.name: Rock Creek Lake to Little Lakes Valley (Family Section)
places.address: Rock Creek Lake, Rock Creek Road, Tom's Place, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: USFS day-use lot at Rock Creek Lake. Small fee. Flush toilets at Rock Creek Lake Resort nearby (summer only). Road open June–October.

places.name: Keough's Hot Springs Road Walk
places.address: Keough Hot Springs Road, Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Free roadside parking at Keough's Hot Springs entrance. Small private facility; walk is on adjacent BLM open land along the road corridor.

places.name: Buttermilk Trail Run Course
places.address: Buttermilk Road, Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Parking at the Buttermilks main area pull-off on Buttermilk Road, 7 miles west of Bishop. Free BLM land. Dirt lot with space for 30+ vehicles.

places.name: Rock Creek Road Run
places.address: Rock Creek Road at US-395 (Tom's Place), CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Park at Tom's Place Resort turnoff on US-395 (free pullout). Run up Rock Creek Road — paved, low traffic in early mornings and evenings. No fee.

places.name: Bishop Creek Canyon Road Run (South Fork)
places.address: South Lake Road / Bishop Creek Canyon, Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Start at the lower Bishop Creek Canyon pullout near the LADWP gate area. Free roadside parking. Run on South Lake Road — paved, minimal traffic on weekday mornings.

places.name: Volcanic Tablelands Loop Run
places.address: Pleasant Valley Dam Road, Bishop, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Free BLM parking at Pleasant Valley Pit area off Pleasant Valley Dam Road. No fee. Vault toilets at campground nearby.

places.name: Piute Pass Trail
places.address: North Lake Trailhead, North Lake Road, Bishop Creek Canyon, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Small USFS dirt lot at North Lake (9,440 ft). ~20 vehicle capacity; arrives full by 7 AM on summer weekends. Vault toilet. Wilderness permit required for overnight (quota applies). Day hike requires no permit.

places.name: Rock Creek to Ruby Lake
places.address: Rock Creek Lake Trailhead, Rock Creek Road, Tom's Place, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: USFS day-use lot at Rock Creek Lake (10,850 ft). Small fee. Vault toilets. Road typically open June–October. No wilderness permit needed for day hike to Ruby Lake.

places.name: Kearsarge Pass Trail
places.address: Onion Valley Trailhead, Onion Valley Road, Independence, CA 93526
places.parking_notes: Large USFS paved parking lot at Onion Valley (9,185 ft). Free. Vault toilets. Popular JMT resupply trailhead; lot fills on summer weekends. Wilderness permit required for overnight into Kings Canyon NP. No permit needed for day hike.

places.name: Buttermilks (Buttermilk Country)
places.address: Buttermilk Road, Bishop, CA 93514 (7 miles west of Bishop on Buttermilk Rd)
places.parking_notes: Free BLM open parking on dirt shoulder along Buttermilk Road. Large informal lot at main boulders accommodates 40+ vehicles. No fee. No restrooms on site. Camp dispersed on BLM land nearby (no developed sites). Road is dirt — high clearance recommended in wet weather.

places.name: Pine Creek Bouldering
places.address: Pine Creek Road, Rovana, CA 93514
places.parking_notes: Park at Pine Creek Pack Station lower lot (fee) or informal pullouts along lower Pine Creek Road. Road is paved to pack station; beyond is dirt. Boulders are on Inyo NF land along the creek.

places.name: Volcanic Tablelands – Fish Slough Sector
places.address: Fish Slough Road, Bishop, CA 93514 (north of Bishop via Five Bridges Rd)
places.parking_notes: Free BLM dispersed parking on Fish Slough Road. No developed facilities. Dirt road accessible to most vehicles in dry conditions. No fee. No restrooms.

places.name: Congress Trail Loop
places.address: Wolverton Road, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Congress Trail / Sherman Tree Parking Lot off Wolverton Rd. Accessible parking available 2 miles north on Generals Highway near Giant Forest Museum. Summer free shuttle from Lodgepole/Wuksachi.

places.name: General Grant Tree Trail
places.address: General Grant Tree Trailhead, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: General Grant Tree Parking Area, 1 mile northwest of Kings Canyon Visitor Center on Hwy 180. Large paved lot, accessible spaces. No shuttle in Grant Grove currently.

places.name: Roaring River Falls
places.address: Roaring River Falls Trailhead, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Designated pull-out parking area 3 miles east of Cedar Grove Village on Hwy 180. Paved, accessible parking. Improved trail paving and reduced grades since 2017.

places.name: Big Trees Trail (Round Meadow Loop)
places.address: Giant Forest Museum, 47050 Generals Hwy, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Accessible parking directly at Giant Forest Museum trailhead. Large paved lot with accessible spaces. Free summer shuttle from Lodgepole/Wuksachi.

places.name: North Grove Loop
places.address: General Grant Tree Trailhead, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Same General Grant Tree Parking Area, 1 mile northwest of Kings Canyon Visitor Center. Large paved lot.

places.name: General Sherman Tree Accessible Path
places.address: Wolverton Rd Trailhead, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Accessible parking at Wolverton Rd lot (near Sherman Tree). Alternatively, accessible parking on Generals Highway 2 miles north of Giant Forest Museum; paved accessible trail leads to tree base. Summer free shuttle stops at Sherman Tree.

places.name: Giant Forest Museum Exhibits & Beetle Rock
places.address: 47050 Generals Hwy, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Large paved lot at Giant Forest Museum with accessible spaces. Free summer shuttle from Lodgepole/Wuksachi. Museum is free to enter.

places.name: Zumwalt Meadow — Family Walk
places.address: Zumwalt Meadow Trailhead, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Dedicated trailhead parking 4.5 miles east of Cedar Grove Village. Arrive early; lot fills quickly in summer.

places.name: Big Stump Loop (Kings Canyon Entrance)
places.address: Big Stump Entrance, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Small parking area just past Big Stump Entrance Station on Hwy 180. Free with park pass. Accessible lot.

places.name: Knapp's Cabin & River Walk (Cedar Grove)
places.address: Knapp's Cabin Turnout, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Roadside turnout/pullout 2 miles east of Cedar Grove Village on Hwy 180. Very small, a few cars. No formal lot.

places.name: Generals Highway Road Run — Ash Mountain to Giant Forest
places.address: Ash Mountain Entrance Station, 47050 Generals Hwy, Three Rivers, CA 93271
places.parking_notes: Park at Foothills Visitor Center near Ash Mountain Entrance. Limited parking at trailheads along the highway. Road can be run as out-and-back or car shuttle to Giant Forest Museum.

places.name: Highway 180 Kings Canyon Scenic Byway Run — Grant Grove to Cedar Grove
places.address: Grant Grove Village, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Park at Grant Grove Village visitor center parking area. Road run on Hwy 180 to Cedar Grove (~30 miles one-way). Best done as car-shuttle. Road open May–November.

places.name: Tokopah Valley Trail Run
places.address: Lodgepole Campground, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Lodgepole day-use parking near visitor center. Pay at kiosk. Trail begins beyond Marble Fork Bridge.

places.name: Mist Falls Trail Run (Road's End, Cedar Grove)
places.address: Road's End Trailhead, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Road's End parking area at end of Hwy 180, past Road's End Permit Station. Large gravel lot. No fee beyond park entry. Open May–November.

places.name: Sequoia Half Marathon (Race Event)
places.address: Three Rivers, CA 93271 (near Sequoia National Park Ash Mountain Entrance)
places.parking_notes: Event-specific parking at designated lots in Three Rivers. Shuttle to start typically provided on race day. Check race organizer for current logistics.

places.name: Big Baldy Ridge Trail Run
places.address: Big Baldy Trailhead, Generals Hwy, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Small pullout / trailhead parking on Generals Highway, 8 miles south of Grant Grove Village. No facilities. Limited spaces.

places.name: Alta Peak via Alta Trail
places.address: Wolverton Trailhead, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Wolverton Parking Area off Wolverton Rd, between Lodgepole and Giant Forest. Large paved lot with restrooms. One of the best-maintained lots in the park.

places.name: Lakes Trail (Four Lakes Loop)
places.address: Wolverton Trailhead, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Wolverton Parking Area off Wolverton Rd. Large paved lot. Popular trailhead; arrive early on weekends.

places.name: Cedar Grove Overlook via Hotel Creek Trail
places.address: Cedar Grove Visitor Center, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Park at Cedar Grove Visitor Center / Village area on Hwy 180. Trail crosses highway from visitor center. Passes through 2024 Happy Fire burn area — check current conditions for downed trees and trail damage.

places.name: Hotel Creek – Lewis Creek Loop
places.address: Cedar Grove Visitor Center, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Park at Cedar Grove Visitor Center / Village. Same trailhead as Cedar Grove Overlook. Passes through 2024 Happy Fire burn area.

places.name: Mist Falls
places.address: Road's End Trailhead, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Road's End parking at end of Hwy 180 past Road's End Permit Station. Large gravel lot. No additional fee beyond park entry. Open May–November.

places.name: Monarch Lakes (Mineral King)
places.address: Sawtooth Trail Parking, Mineral King Rd, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Sawtooth/Monarch Lakes trailhead parking area 1 mile past Mineral King Ranger Station at end of Mineral King Rd. Very narrow, steep, winding road — RVs and trailers prohibited. 25-mile drive from Hwy 198 taking ~1.5 hours. Marmots damage vehicles; use fencing/chicken wire provided or wrap wheel wells.

places.name: Buena Vista Peak Bouldering
places.address: Buena Vista Peak Trailhead, Generals Hwy, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Small trailhead parking area just south of Kings Canyon Overlook on Generals Highway, 6 miles southeast of Grant Grove Village. A few cars; roadside pullout. No facilities.

places.name: Moro Rock — West Face Rock Climbing & Bouldering
places.address: Moro Rock Trailhead, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Small Moro Rock parking area 2 miles off Generals Hwy on Moro Rock/Crescent Meadow Rd. Summer shuttle recommended. May close during peregrine falcon nesting season (seasonal closure — check NPS current conditions before visiting).

places.name: Hospital Rock Boulders (Foothills)
places.address: Hospital Rock Picnic Area, Generals Hwy, Sequoia National Park, CA 93271
places.parking_notes: Hospital Rock Picnic Area parking off Generals Highway in the Foothills area (~6 miles from Ash Mountain Entrance). Paved parking with restrooms and water. Day use only.

places.name: Tokopah Domes — Lodgepole Area
places.address: Lodgepole Campground, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Park at Lodgepole Visitor Center / campground lot. Approach granite domes via Tokopah Falls trail or cross-country. Wilderness ethic required off-trail.

places.name: Bubbs Creek Wall — Charlotte Dome Bouldering Approach
places.address: Road's End Trailhead, Cedar Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, CA 93633
places.parking_notes: Road's End parking at end of Hwy 180 in Cedar Grove. Large gravel lot. Backcountry permit required for overnight trips. Day access for the first few miles is permit-free.

places.name: Little Baldy Granite Dome — Scrambling & Bouldering
places.address: Little Baldy Saddle Trailhead, Generals Hwy, Sequoia National Park, CA 93262
places.parking_notes: Small pullout / parking area at Little Baldy Saddle on Generals Highway between Grant Grove and Lodgepole. Limited to a few cars. No facilities.

places.name: Taylor Creek Stream Trail
places.address: Taylor Creek Visitor Center, 870 Emerald Bay Rd, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
places.parking_notes: Free parking at Taylor Creek Visitor Center (USFS). Fills on summer weekends. Flush toilets on site.

places.name: Emerald Bay State Park — Shoreline Walk
places.address: Emerald Bay State Park, Emerald Bay Rd (Hwy 89), South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
places.parking_notes: Emerald Bay Overlook parking on Hwy 89 (day use fee). Shuttle from South Lake Tahoe recommended in summer — lot fills by 9 AM. Vikingsholm parking lot is lower and larger.

places.name: Cascade Falls
places.address: Bayview Campground Trailhead, Hwy 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
places.parking_notes: Bayview Campground parking area on east side of Hwy 89. Day use parking on shoulder. No fee for day use at trailhead. Fills quickly on summer weekends.

places.name: Phantom Spires Bouldering — El Dorado NF
places.address: Phantom Spires Trailhead, Ice House Rd, El Dorado National Forest, CA 95726
places.parking_notes: Small parking pullout on Ice House Road, El Dorado National Forest. No fee. Rough road access — high clearance recommended.

places.name: D.L. Bliss State Park — Shoreline Walk
places.address: D.L. Bliss State Park, Hwy 89, Tahoma, CA 96142
places.parking_notes: D.L. Bliss State Park entrance off Hwy 89. Day use fee. Rubicon Trail parking near Calawee Cove Beach.

places.name: Taylor Creek Stream Trail Trailhead
places.address: Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (Taylor Creek Visitor Center, 3.5 mi north of SLT city limits)
places.parking_notes: Free day-use parking lot at Taylor Creek Visitor Center off Hwy 89. Fills early in fall during salmon run season (Oct).

places.name: Emerald Bay State Park – Vikingsholm Trailhead Parking
places.address: Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (12 mi north of South Lake Tahoe, 22 mi south of Tahoe City)
places.parking_notes: Vikingsholm parking lot off Hwy 89. $10/day or $3/hr. Max vehicle length 18 ft. Fills by mid-morning in summer. Emerald Bay Shuttle available at Eagle Falls Trailhead through Oct 15 as overflow alternative.

places.name: Eagle Falls Trailhead
places.address: Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (adjacent to Emerald Bay, 0.3 mi north of Vikingsholm parking)
places.parking_notes: USFS fee area parking lot off Hwy 89. Fee applies. Shuttle pickup point for Emerald Bay Shuttle (through Oct 15). Dogs allowed (USFS land). Very limited spaces – arrive before 8 am on weekends.

places.name: Cascade Falls Trailhead
places.address: Cascade Road off Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (near Cascade Lake, south of Emerald Bay)
places.parking_notes: Small roadside parking area on Cascade Rd near Hwy 89. Free, no fee. Limited spaces (~10-15 cars). No facilities. Overflow park along Hwy 89 shoulder.

places.name: Mt. Tallac Trailhead
places.address: Mt. Tallac Rd off Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (3.5 mi north of South Lake Tahoe on Hwy 89, left onto Mt. Tallac Rd)
places.parking_notes: Free USFS parking lot at end of Mt. Tallac Rd. ~30 spaces. No fee for day use. Desolation Wilderness day-use permit required at trailhead self-issue station. Fills by 7 am on summer weekends.

places.name: Glen Alpine Trailhead (Desolation Wilderness)
places.address: Glen Alpine Rd, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (off Fallen Leaf Lake Rd, El Dorado National Forest)
places.parking_notes: Free USFS parking at Glen Alpine Springs area. ~20 spaces. Self-issue Desolation Wilderness permit at trailhead. Fallen Leaf Lake Rd from Hwy 89; continue past Fallen Leaf Lake to road end.

places.name: Echo Lakes Trailhead (Lower Echo Lake)
places.address: Johnson Pass Rd off Highway 50, Echo Summit, CA 95762 (Echo Lakes Rd from Hwy 50, 1 mi north of Echo Summit)
places.parking_notes: USFS fee parking lot at Lower Echo Lake. $10/day. Water taxi available across Echo Lakes in summer (paid service). Desolation Wilderness permit required for Wilderness entry. Tahoe Rim Trail trailhead also here.

places.name: Fallen Leaf Lake Road Trailhead Area
places.address: 2165 Fallen Leaf Rd, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
places.parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along Fallen Leaf Lake Rd. Glen Alpine Trailhead at road end has ~20 free spaces. Fallen Leaf Lake Campground (recreation.gov reservations) adjacent. No day-use fee for road walk.

places.name: Pope Beach / Camp Richardson Shoreline
places.address: Pope Beach Rd off Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (adjacent to Camp Richardson Resort)
places.parking_notes: USFS Pope Beach day-use parking lot, fee area ($10–$15/day). Camp Richardson Resort parking also available with lodging/dining. Paved lot, restrooms, beach access. Very busy July–Aug.

places.name: Ralston Peak Trailhead
places.address: Highway 50, Echo Summit, CA 95762 (just west of Echo Summit, Hwy 50 pullout)
places.parking_notes: Small roadside pullout on Hwy 50 at Echo Summit area. ~10 vehicles. No fee. Desolation Wilderness day permit required (self-issue at trailhead). No facilities.

places.name: Bayview Trailhead (Granite Lake / Desolation Wilderness)
places.address: Bayview Campground, Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (10 mi north of SLT, near Emerald Bay)
places.parking_notes: Bayview Campground day-use parking off Hwy 89. Small lot ~15 cars. USFS. Desolation Wilderness day permit required (self-issue). Campground adjacent (recreation.gov reservations).

places.name: Lake Tahoe Marathon – Start/Finish (Cave Rock Area)
places.address: Cave Rock, Highway 50, Zephyr Cove, NV 89448 (south shore Lake Tahoe, annual event varies location)
places.parking_notes: Event parking at designated areas for race day. Free participant parking. Check race-specific instructions each year. South shore lake corridor.

places.name: Big Meadow Trailhead (Tahoe Rim Trail)
places.address: Luther Pass Rd (Hwy 89), Meyers, CA 96150 (3 mi south of SLT on Hwy 89 near Luther Pass)
places.parking_notes: Free USFS parking area off Hwy 89 at Big Meadow. ~25 spaces. No fee. TRT trailhead signage. No permit required for this TRT section. Restrooms at trailhead.

places.name: Lover's Leap Climbing Area
places.address: Lover's Leap Rd off Highway 50, Strawberry, CA 95684 (El Dorado National Forest, ~7 mi east of Kyburz, ~25 mi from South Lake Tahoe)
places.parking_notes: Free USFS gravel parking area along Hwy 50 and Lover's Leap Rd near Strawberry. No fee. No reservations. Pit toilet on site. 30–40 spaces. Can fill on summer/fall weekends.

places.name: D.L. Bliss State Park – Rubicon Trail Trailhead
places.address: D.L. Bliss State Park, Highway 89, Tahoma, CA 96142 (17 mi north of South Lake Tahoe on Hwy 89)
places.parking_notes: State Park day-use lot. $10–$12/day. NOTE: D.L. Bliss State Park closed to vehicles and pedestrians in 2025 for construction. Check parks.ca.gov for current status. Rubicon Trail currently accessed from Emerald Bay end only.

places.name: Emerald Bay Overlook / Inspiration Point
places.address: Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (Vikingsholm parking lot area, 12 mi north of SLT)
places.parking_notes: Shared with Vikingsholm Trail parking. $10/day or $3/hr. Pull-off viewpoint also available 0.2 mi north on Hwy 89 (very limited, 3–4 cars). The overlook itself is 0.1 mi flat walk from Vikingsholm lot.

places.name: Heavenly Mountain – Tamarack Trail (Summer)
places.address: 3860 Saddle Rd, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (Heavenly Village Gondola base)
places.parking_notes: Heavenly Village parking structures and lots in downtown South Lake Tahoe. $10–$30/day depending on lot. Gondola fee required to access upper mountain trails ($35–$45 adult). Free viewing area at base.

places.name: Angora Ridge Boulder Field (near Fallen Leaf Lake)
places.address: Angora Ridge Rd off Fallen Leaf Lake Rd, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (El Dorado National Forest / LTBMU)
places.parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Angora Ridge Rd. Free, no fee. High-clearance vehicle recommended for upper road. ~5–8 cars at various pullouts. Angora Lookout (fire lookout) at road end provides additional parking.

places.name: Pyramid Creek Trailhead (Horsetail Falls / Desolation Wilderness)
places.address: Highway 50, Twin Bridges, CA 95735 (3 mi west of Echo Summit on Hwy 50)
places.parking_notes: USFS paid parking area on Hwy 50 at Twin Bridges. $5–$10/day. Very busy in summer; fills by 9 am on weekends. Desolation Wilderness day permit required. Restrooms on site.

places.name: Kiva Beach (USFS Day-Use Area)
places.address: Kiva Beach Rd off Highway 89, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150 (2 mi north of SLT city limits, near LTBMU Visitor Center)
places.parking_notes: USFS Kiva Beach day-use lot off Hwy 89. Fee area. Connects via shoreline path to Camp Richardson (1.5 mi west) and to South Lake Tahoe city beach (2 mi east). Restrooms and picnic area on site.

places.name: Echo Summit Roadside Bouldering
places.address: Echo Summit, Hwy 50, El Dorado County, CA 95726
places.parking_notes: Pullouts along Hwy 50 at Echo Summit (7,382 ft). Multiple small areas. Free. Restrooms at Echo Summit Sno-Park in winter.

places.name: Big Chief Crag — Bouldering Base
places.address: Alpine Meadows Rd, Olympic Valley, CA 96146
places.parking_notes: Roadside pullout on Alpine Meadows Rd just off Hwy 89, ~0.3 mi from junction. Free, no permit. Tahoe National Forest land.

places.name: Pie Shop Crag — Bouldering
places.address: Hwy 50 (US-50), Twin Bridges, CA 95735
places.parking_notes: Roadside pullout on south side of US-50 at Twin Bridges, El Dorado National Forest. Free, no fee. 0.2 mi walk to Pie Shop wall.

places.name: Pywiack Dome — Tuolumne Bouldering
places.address: Tenaya Lake East Parking, Tioga Rd (Hwy 120), Yosemite NP, CA 95389
places.parking_notes: East Tenaya Lake picnic lot on Tioga Rd. Free with park entrance fee. 0.4 mi walk to Pywiack Dome base. Summer shuttle stop nearby.

activities.notes: Paved 0.2-mi path descends 100 ft to geothermal pools. Swimming and bathing prohibited. Stay on walkways. Closed sunset–sunrise. Extraordinary geology; excellent for all ages with supervision.
Flat loop around Lake Mary at 8,895 ft elevation. Paved and dirt sections. Stunning alpine scenery, good fishing access. Part of Mammoth Lakes Basin.

2-mile loop hugging Convict Lake shoreline. ADA-accessible paved section between marina and parking. Boardwalk bridge over Convict Creek. Short hill on northeast shore. Popular with families and anglers. Dramatic granite walls of Mt. Morrison overhead.

Rolling 5-mile (8 km) round-trip hike to 101-ft Rainbow Falls, tallest waterfall on Middle Fork San Joaquin River. Pumice trail through lodgepole forest. Can be hot and dry in summer—bring water. Return via shuttle stops 9 or 10. No pets on trail.

Easy 0.8-mile round trip to base of 60-ft columnar basalt columns; add 15-min uphill spur to summit view with glacial polish. One of world's finest examples of columnar basalt. Excellent for all ages.
Easy 1.4-mile round trip to small cascading waterfall just outside NM boundary. Forested trail alongside San Joaquin River. Short and family-appropriate.
Paved multi-use path connecting Mammoth Lakes town center to Lakes Basin area. Ideal for strollers, casual walkers, and cyclists. Gentle grades. Town of Mammoth Lakes maintained.
Short connector walk between Lake George (9,000 ft) and Lake Mary (8,895 ft) through lodgepole pine. Lakeside scenery, gentle grades. Good warm-up walk in the basin.
Same loop as walk entry; highlighted here for family audiences. ADA-accessible paved section. Fishing, picnicking, and wildlife watching. Excellent for young children. Classic Mammoth family outing.
Paved path to geothermal hot springs and boiling pools. Great educational stop for kids—volcanic geology visible. Adult supervision required near thermal hazards. No swimming.
Flat loop around scenic alpine lake at 8,895 ft. Good fishing and picnicking. Restrooms at campground. Crowded on summer weekends; arrive early.
Fun geology lesson—hexagonal basalt columns fascinate kids. Very short, very easy. Junior Ranger program available at visitor center. Top of columns accessible via short uphill spur.

101-ft waterfall on Middle Fork San Joaquin River. Moderately long for young children but the destination is spectacular. Rolling terrain on pumice trail. Shuttle return available at stops 9–10. Bring water—hot and sunny.
Short lakeside walk around Lake George at 9,000 ft. Beautiful views of Crystal Crag. Fishing access. Very accessible for young children.
Easy 1.4-mile round trip to small waterfall. Shaded forest trail alongside river. Short enough for toddlers with rest breaks. Great introduction to Reds Meadow Valley.

3 miles / 2,400 ft vertical straight up Mammoth Mountain to 11,053-ft summit. Technical singletrack at high altitude. Orange flags. Annual race event, September. Free gondola descent after. Prize purse $3,400.

32 miles with ~7,000 ft gain. Avg elevation 9,100 ft; lowest 8,050 ft; highest 11,053 ft. Single loop—no out-and-backs. Singletrack, pumice, bike path, seven alpine lakes. Finishes at Village after Dragon's Back summit. Annual event Sept (Saturday). 12-hr cutoff.

16.1 miles / 3,900+ ft gain. Elevation range 8,050–11,053 ft. Single loop, no reused trail. Singletrack, pumice, rocky alpine terrain, summit of Mammoth Mountain. Annual event Sept (Sunday). 7-hr cutoff; 97% finish rate in 2025.

~5.9 miles; ~800 ft gain/loss. After-dark party race at 6:45 PM (Friday). Climbs Uptown Trail to summit, descends Downtown Trail. Marked with green flags and reflective tape, disco lights at aid stations. 'Perfect entry level event.' Cutoff 9 PM.

3-day stage race: Dragon's Back Ascent (Fri) + 50K (Sat) + 26K (Sun). Total 51 miles / 13,400+ ft gain. Golden Trail Series event. Top-3 per category awards at Village Plaza on Sunday. Random drug testing applies.

1 km kids fun run at Mammoth Trail Fest (Saturday morning, 9:30 AM). Open to children 70 inches/feet tall and taller. Village start through trail section. Course map on CalTopo. Part of family festival activities.

Popular local training route linking Lake Mary, Lake George, Crystal Lake, Horseshoe Lake, and Mammoth Pass. Mix of singletrack and fire road. Altitude 8,900–9,700 ft. Non-motorized summer trail. No designated race—local running route.

9 miles round trip to Duck Pass (10,797 ft) via Arrowhead, Skelton, and Horse lakes. Sustained climb through John Muir Wilderness. Stunning high alpine terrain. Wilderness permit required for overnight. Best July–Oct.

3.5-mile round trip from Lake George to Crystal Lake (9,512 ft) below Crystal Crag. Steady switchback climb through lodgepole. Glacially scoured basin. Moderately challenging. Best July–Oct. Wilderness permit not required for day hike.

7.1-mile trail along Convict Creek into John Muir Wilderness. Passes Mildred Lake at 5 miles with option to continue to Lake Dorothy (10,275 ft), Lake Genevieve, and Edith Lake. Dramatic canyon scenery. Bears active—canister required overnight.

5-mile round trip to a series of small alpine lakes (9,500–9,900 ft) below Sherwin Range. Popular local trail with moderate sustained climb. John Muir Wilderness. Best July–Oct. Bear canisters recommended for overnight.
4-mile round trip from Lake George to Barrett Lake (9,648 ft) and optional TJ Lake. Off-trail scramble section to TJ Lake. Quieter than Crystal Lake trail. Spectacular views. Best July–Oct.

4.2-mile trail starting at ~9,910 ft. Immediately enters John Muir Wilderness. Passes through dramatic terrain to Edith Lake. Optional extension to Lake Genevieve and Lake Dorothy. Less-traveled alternative to Lakes Basin trails.

Extended Lakes Basin hike combining Crystal Lake trail with ridge scramble to Crystal Crag saddle (10,377 ft) for panoramic views of Mammoth Lakes Basin. Uses Crystal Lake trail then climbs cross-country. Best for experienced hikers.

Volcanic rhyolite bouldering at ~8,000 ft on the slopes below Mammoth Mountain. Problems range V0–V7. Best in summer and fall when lowland crags are too hot. Short approach. Multiple sectors across the hillside. Inyo National Forest land.

In-town granite and volcanic bouldering scattered through the Jeffrey pine forest of Shady Rest Park/Inyo National Forest. Accessible year-round when not snowed in. Problems V0–V5. Popular local training area; relaxed atmosphere suitable for beginners and families.

Scattered granite and basalt boulders in the Reds Meadow valley near Devils Postpile. Access via mandatory shuttle mid-June–early Sept. Pumice soil approach. High-altitude (7,600 ft) alpine setting. V0–V6 range. Less crowded than town areas.

Granite boulders scattered around Lakes Basin near Lake George and Lake Mary at 8,900–9,200 ft. High-quality Sierra granite. Problems V0–V8. Short approach from parking. Inyo National Forest land. Best July–Oct when snow-free.

Volcanic and rhyolite bouldering near Deadman Creek drainage ~15 miles north of Mammoth on Hwy 395. Multiple scattered boulder fields; some problems V0–V7. Extended season—often accessible when Lakes Basin snowbound. Inyo National Forest.
Crystal-clear water walk on boardwalk and sand. East Shore's most scenic beach. Early morning arrival essential summer weekends. Wheelchair accessible boardwalk.
2-mile flat loop around Spooner Lake at 7,000 ft. Aspen groves, wildflowers July-Aug. Good birding. Mountain bike share trail—stay alert. Popular with families.
Paved lakefront promenade with pier access. Swimming beach with lifeguards in summer. Picnic areas, playground nearby. Excellent for strollers.
Paved 4-mile (6.4 km) multi-use trail along Truckee River from Tahoe City to Squaw Valley. Gentle grade. River beaches for wading. Interpretive signs. Excellent for bikes, strollers, walking.
Short trail through pine forest to secluded beach. Sandy coves for wading, rock skipping. Less crowded than Sand Harbor. Good for kids 3+.
Short lakeside stroll to historic Carnelian Bay pier. Fishing, sunset viewing. Nearby playground at Patton Landing Beach. Can combine with beach access.
5-mile loop combining TRT with old fire roads. Singletrack with lake views, wildflower meadows. 8,300 ft elevation—acclimate before hard effort. Popular with trail runners.

Annual Tahoe City Marathon course (October). Mostly paved along lakefront and bike paths. Gentle rolling hills. Self-guided route any time—follow bike path network clockwise around lake for shorter options.
Alpine running at 6,200+ ft. Summer trail race series (5K/10K). Mix of singletrack and fire road. Excellent training at altitude. Chairlift access for downhill options.
2-mile round trip to alpine Eagle Lake above Emerald Bay. Steep switchbacks through granite. Lake swimming, granite slabs for lunch. Connects to Desolation Wilderness.
9.5-mile round trip to 9,735 ft summit. Best panoramic views in Tahoe region. 360-degree views of Lake Tahoe, Desolation Wilderness, Sierra Crest. Strenuous—start early, bring water.
1.5-mile round trip to Cascade Falls and Lake. Family-friendly with granite stream features. Waterfall best in late spring/early summer. Cool off in granite pools.
Granite bouldering at 7,200 ft. Classics: 'Girls Gone Wild' (V1), 'Pimpsqueak' (V5). Best conditions July-Sept. Bring pads. Multiple areas along 1-mile stretch.
Glacial erratics and small crags. Grades V0-V7. Best spring/fall. Combine with hike to avoid crowds at Donner. Need local beta—less documented than Donner.
2.5-mile loop on old fire road and singletrack. Views of Lake Tahoe and Heavenly Valley. Connector to Tahoe Rim Trail. Good option near town with moderate effort.
3-mile loop along Fallen Leaf Lake shore. Quiet alternative to busy Lake Tahoe beaches. Granite boulders for scrambling. Less crowded than main lake. Non-motorized only.
Paved pedestrian village with shops, restaurants, fountains for kids. Gondola base. Not wilderness but good family walk combined with gondola ride for mountain views.
1.5-mile sandy beach walk with picnic areas. Gentle lake entry for kids. Buoys mark swimming area. Restrooms and concessions in summer. Less crowded than Nevada Beach.
7.5-mile out-and-back on TRT with 1,600 ft gain. High ridge running with lake views. Singletrack with some technical sections. Start early—exposed after 9am.
Annual Lake Tahoe Marathon (October) with 5K, half, full, 72-mile ultra options. Course around lake on bike paths and trails. Self-guided training routes available year-round.
5.3-mile loop combining Rubicon Trail and road sections. Scenic singletrack above Emerald Bay. Technical in sections. Best fall colors September-October.
6-mile round trip to Maggies Peaks with Desolation Wilderness access. Panoramic views of Fallen Leaf Lake and Lake Tahoe. Granite slabs, wildflowers. Class 2 scramble to true summit.
7.5-mile round trip to Upper Echo Lake and beyond. Boardwalk trail along lakes. Connects to PCT/JMT. Alpine scenery, granite peaks. Boat taxi cuts 2.5 miles each way.
1.4-mile round trip to Vikingsholm mansion (Swedish castle replica) on Emerald Bay shore. Historic home tours in summer. Steep paved path. Beautiful bay views.

World-famous granite crag at 5,720 ft on South Fork American River near Strawberry, El Dorado NF. Primarily sport/trad climbing (5.6–5.12) but excellent bouldering on lower crags and approach slabs. V0–V8. Best season April–November. BLM/USFS access — no permit required. ~45 min from South Lake Tahoe.
1.2-mile round trip on old road bed to carbonated mineral springs and historic Parsons Memorial Lodge. ADA-accessible boardwalk. Interpretive signs. One of Tuolumne's easiest walks at 8,600 ft.
2.5-mile gravel path along Tuolumne River through subalpine meadow. PCT/JMT junction area. Wildflowers July-August. River access. Generally flat with minimal elevation change.
1-mile easy loop on old mining road to Parsons Lodge (Sierra Club-built, 1915). Soda Springs, boardwalks. Interpretive programs some days. Excellent for children—nature at eye level.
1.5-mile round trip to granite dome summit with Tuolumne River views. Easy scramble (Class 1). Glacial polish. Wildflowers in cracks. Gentle introduction to Sierra scrambling.
6.2-mile out-and-back along Tuolumne River into Lyell Canyon. Gradual 200 ft gain on PCT/JMT tread. World-class trail running at 8,600-8,900 ft. Best conditions July-September.
8-mile loop to Lower and Upper Cathedral Lakes at 9,200 ft. Steep initial 2 miles (1,000 ft gain). Alpine lakes beneath Cathedral Peak. Technical rocky sections. Best July-October.
12-mile out-and-back on JMT/PCT from Tuolumne Meadows to Cathedral Lakes junction. Classic Sierra high country running. Mix of meadow and forest. Elevation 8,600-9,400 ft.
2.8-mile round trip with Class 2 scramble to 9,450 ft summit. 360° views of Tuolumne Meadows, Sierra Crest. Exposed summit—use caution with children. Glacial erratics.
7-mile round trip to Lower Cathedral Lake (9,200 ft). Steep initial 3 miles. Stunning reflection of Cathedral Peak. Camping available with permit. Continue 1 mile to Upper Lake for solitude.
6.2-mile round trip to 13,061 ft Mount Dana, Yosemite's second highest peak. Trail starts at 9,943 ft (Tioga Pass). Class 1-2 scramble. Breathtaking views of Mono Lake. Start before 7am.
Granite bouldering at 8,700 ft. Classics: 'Bachar-Yerian' (V2), 'Nemesis' (V6). Knobby granite. Best July-September. Limited landings—bring multiple pads. Part of famous Camp 4-style scene.
1.2-mile paved loop to base of Lower Yosemite Fall (320 ft). ADA accessible. Views of both Upper (1,430 ft) and Lower falls. Spray can be intense in spring. Open year-round.
1-mile flat boardwalk through meadow with views of Half Dome, Yosemite Falls, El Capitan. Best photography at golden hour. Wheelchair accessible. Interpretive signs about valley ecology.
2.5-mile paved and dirt loop to seasonal Mirror Lake. Best in spring/early summer when full. Reflections of Half Dome. Sandy beach areas. ADA accessible to first viewpoint.
0.2-mile walk to pedestrian suspension bridge over Merced River. Views of Yosemite Falls. Sandy beach below bridge for wading. Quiet alternative to crowded spots. Excellent for toddlers.
Short riverside walk at iconic view of El Capitan, Bridalveil Fall, and Cathedral Rocks. One of Ansel Adams' famous photo locations. Picnic spots on sandbars. Gentle grade for all ages.

1.5-mile round trip paved trail to 620-ft waterfall base. Wet and slippery from spray year-round. Wind can push spray dramatically. Beautiful rainbows in afternoon. Accessible but steep final section.
7-mile loop on paved bike path and dirt trails around valley floor. Mix of forest, meadow, and river. Elevation 4,000 ft. Good training run with iconic views throughout. Best at sunrise.
Annual Yosemite Half Marathon (May). Paved and dirt roads through valley with views of all major formations. 4,000 ft elevation. Self-guided training routes available year-round on similar terrain.
6-mile round trip to Glacier Point. 3,200 ft gain—one of the biggest in the valley. Singletrack switchbacks with epic views. Run up, shuttle down or reverse. Best April-November.
3.2-mile round trip to 317-ft Vernal Fall. 'Mist' from waterfall drenches hikers—bring rain gear. Granite steps (600+ of them). Spectacular in spring runoff. Slippery when wet.
6.5-mile round trip past Vernal to 594-ft Nevada Fall. Continue past John Muir Trail junction. 'Giant Staircase' of granite steps. Dry sections by late summer. Combine with JMT for loop.

7.2-mile round trip to top of 2,425-ft Yosemite Falls (North America's tallest). Extremely steep switchbacks. Columbia Rock viewpoint at 1 mile. Seasonal—waterfall dry late summer. Avoid ice in winter.
17-mile round trip via Mist Trail or JMT. 4,800 ft total gain. Iconic 400-ft cable section to 8,839 ft summit. Permit via lottery ($10 + $10/person). Start before 5am. Seasonal cables May-October.

World-famous granite bouldering birthplace. Classics: 'Midnight Lightning' (V8), 'Cocaine Corner' (V2). Free camping area. Historic climbing culture. Bring multiple crash pads. Best spring/fall—summer hot.
Historic bouldering area with easier problems. Grades V0-V4. Great for beginners to intermediate. Better shade than Camp 4 in summer. Mix of slab and vertical granite.
3-mile paved urban trail along Bishop Creek through town. Multiple access points. Interpretive signs on local history and ecology. Links to parks and shopping. Pleasant even in midday heat.
2-mile lakeside walk on service road. Fishing access, aspen groves. Part of Bishop Creek Canyon system. Good intro to Eastern Sierra at 7,600 ft. Fall colors spectacular in October.
1-mile stroll around Weir Lake at 9,000 ft. Small lake with aspen and willow. Good fishing. Quiet alternative to larger South Lake. Often frozen until June.
1.5-mile out-and-back on old mining road. Interpretive signs on 1870s mining. Gentle grade suitable for young children. Views of Owens Valley. Can combine with pack station visit for kids.
1.2-mile lakeside stroll at 9,128 ft. Dam views, wildflower meadows July-Aug. High altitude—watch for altitude sickness in kids. Fishing pier. Boat rentals available.
1-mile interpretive loop at 10,000 ft among 4,000+ year old bristlecone pines (world's oldest trees). ADA accessible boardwalk option. Amazing high desert alpine environment. Start slow at altitude.
6.2-mile loop combining Bishop Pass Trail and lake roads. 7,800-9,200 ft elevation. Trail and dirt road mix. Aspen groves, Sierra peaks. Best June-October. Bear country—be aware.
5-mile out-and-back on dirt roads and trails through famous bouldering area. 5,200 ft elevation with views of Owens Valley. Connects to old mining roads. Morning best—afternoon heat.
Annual Bishop High Sierra 50K/100K ultra (September). Routes through pine forests and high desert. 4,000-8,000 ft elevation. Race events; similar training routes available on local trails year-round.
11-mile round trip to Bishop Pass (11,972 ft). Stunning alpine lakes (Long, Saddlerock, Ruwau). JMT/PCT access. High Sierra peaks. Often snow until July. Thunderstorm risk afternoons.
8-mile round trip through spectacular canyon to alpine basin at 11,200 ft. Waterfalls in spring. Abandoned tungsten mine ruins. Pass connects to wilderness beyond. Best July-September.
6-mile round trip off-trail scramble to 12,237 ft summit. Cross-country navigation skills required. Views of entire Bishop Creek basin. Peak bagging destination. Bring map and compass.
7.5-mile round trip to Meysan Lake below Mt Whitney. Eastern Sierra dramatic peaks. Less crowded than main Whitney trail. 8,000-11,000 ft elevation. Stunning views of Alabama Hills.
World-famous granite bouldering at 5,500 ft. 20+ distinct areas, 1000+ problems. Classics: 'Birthday Direct' (V3), 'Harvey' (V4). Featured in climbing films. Best Oct-May (summer hot). Bring 3+ pads.
Volcanic tuff bouldering (different rock type than Buttermilks). Higher density of problems, more shade. Grades V0-V10+. Often less crowded than Buttermilks. Good for mid-day sessions. Bring pads.
Adjacent to Happy Boulders. Similar volcanic tuff. Technical, crimpy problems. V2-V10 range. Good for working technique. Less crowded than main Buttermilk areas. Approach through sagebrush.
0.6-mile paved loop to world's largest tree by volume (52,500 cu ft). Giant Forest wheelchair accessible. Fallen monarch tunnel. Information plaques. Most visited site in park.
1-mile paved interpretive loop through General Grant Grove. Second largest tree (General Grant). Open year-round—sometimes snow in winter. Holiday tree lighting ceremony each December.
1.5-mile riverside stroll along South Fork Kings River. Canyon floor at 4,600 ft. Sandy beaches for wading. Views of Grand Sentinel and North Dome. Best April-November.
1.3-mile paved loop on meadow edge with views of massive sequoias. ADA accessible. Audio tour available. Ranger programs at museum. Sunset views of sequoias against meadow.
Short walk to fallen sequoia once used as auto tunnel (now too decayed to drive on). Interpretive signs. Historic 1920s tourist attraction. Combine with Moro Rock climb.
1.5-mile boardwalk and trail through meadow with Kings River. Scenic heart of canyon. Suspension bridge crossing. Wildflowers June-July. Best views of granite canyon walls.
1.5-mile loop through sequoia forest to Circle Meadow. Views of Moro Rock from forest. Log bridges, wildflowers. Quiet alternative to Sherman Tree crowds. Giant sequoias throughout.
6.2-mile loop combining meadow trails and logging road connectors. 6,500 ft elevation. Sequoias, meadows, historic sites. Singletrack and old road mix. Best April-November.
5-mile out-and-back along South Fork Kings River. Canyon floor at 4,600 ft. Flat to gentle grade. Sandy beaches for cool-off. Views of canyon walls. Shaded sections.
0.5-mile climb to 6,725 ft dome summit. 400 steps carved in granite. Exposed stairs with handrails. Panoramic views of Great Western Divide, San Joaquin Valley. Fear of heights caution.
3.7-mile round trip to 1,200-ft falls on Kaweah River. Trail along river through lodgepole pine. Dramatic canyon walls. Falls may be dry by late summer. Early morning best for shade.
9-mile round trip along South Fork Kings River to Mist Falls. Gradual climb through canyon. Falls spray visible from trail. Continue to Paradise Valley for backpacking. Best April-October.
11-mile round trip to Heather and Aster Lakes. Enters the Great Western Divide. High Sierra lakes, granite peaks. Elevation 7,200-10,400 ft. Often snow into July. Multi-day backpacking possible.
41-mile backpacking loop (multiple days) through some of Sierra's finest high country. Alpine lakes, passes over 11,000 ft, John Muir Trail section. Rae Lakes, Glen Pass. Permit required.

4-mile round trip to 8,019 ft summit via PCT. Well-maintained trail, no scrambling. Views of Donner Lake, Sierra Crest. Good first peak—clear trail, obvious summit. Some granite slabs near top but walkable.

5-mile loop combining PCT and Pacific Crest alternate. Summit 8,243 ft. Old volcanic terrain, great views of Donner Summit area. Trail well-marked. Some loose rock on final approach but non-technical. Excellent intro to Sierra scrambling.

7-mile round trip to 9,235 ft. Steep but well-defined trail through forest to granite summit. 360° views of Lake Tahoe and Desolation Wilderness. Last 200 yards are loose scree—take your time. Good fitness test peak.

2.5-mile round trip to 10,779 ft—highest peak in Tahoe basin. Trail from summit parking. Gradual grade, wide switchbacks. Dramatic views of Lake Tahoe. Often snow until July. Good for building altitude tolerance. No scrambling required.

1.2-mile round trip to granite dome summit at 8,700 ft. Class 1 walking—no real scrambling. Tuolumne River views, meadow panorama. Perfect first dome for kids/families. Can link with meadow walk for longer day.

4-mile out-and-back to Marmot Dome (9,800 ft) on way to Young Lakes. Cross-country last 0.3 miles but obvious route. Class 1-2. Views of Tuolumne Meadows and surrounding peaks. Good practice for cross-country navigation. Continue to Young Lakes for full day.

5-mile round trip to 10,377 ft via Duck Pass Trail then fork to Crystal Lake/Crag. Moderately steep forest trail, then talus field to summit block. Class 2 scrambling on granite final 100 yards. Not technical but requires route-finding. Views of Mammoth Lakes basin.

4.5-mile round trip to 11,600 ft via old road then cross-country ridge walk. Spectacular views of Ritter Range, Minarets, Banner/Ritter peaks. Walkable ridge—no scrambling. Good intro to cross-country travel with obvious goal. Windy on ridge.

2.5-mile round trip to 9,400 ft volcanic rock summit. Western approach is walkable—eastern side has cliffs to avoid. Good views of Mammoth Mountain. Trail fades near top but cairns mark route. Used for both hiking and bouldering access.

7-mile round trip to 11,682 ft. Cross-country after Chocolate Lakes. Class 2 on loose talus. North side only—south face is technical. Views of entire Bishop Creek basin, Hurd Peak, Dusy Basin approach. Good practice with loose rock.

6-mile round trip to 12,600+ ft false summit area on Mt Tom shoulder. Old mining road then trail. Not true summit but high point with excellent views of Owens Valley, White Mountains. Good for acclimatization. Route continues to true summit but requires Class 2+.

8.7-mile round trip to 7,542 ft dome with 360° views of Half Dome, Valley, Yosemite Falls. Trail to Yosemite Falls, then traverse to North Dome. Mostly walking with brief Class 2 section. One of best valley viewpoints without technical climbing. Return via Indian Rock or same route.

2-mile round trip to 8,122 ft granite dome summit. Paved to dirt trail then granite walk-up—no scrambling required. Arguably best easy summit in Yosemite: views of Half Dome, El Capitan, Yosemite Falls, entire valley. Accessible to most fitness levels. Sunset spectacular.

2-mile easy walk to 7,503 ft viewpoint—more viewpoint than peak but included for perspective. Dramatic vertical cliff edge (fissures) with valley views. Flat trail, minimal elevation. Good for building comfort with heights. Keep children back from cliff edge—serious exposure here.

4-mile round trip to 7,600+ ft summit via old fire road then short cross-country. Views of western divide, Central Valley. Often overlooked peak in Giant Forest area. Class 1 walking. Good for practicing map reading—trail not always obvious.

0.4-mile paved path to 7,520 ft viewpoint—more accessible summit experience. 360° views of Great Western Divide, Sierra Crest. Good for beginners, children, those testing altitude. Interpretive signs. Combine with Grant Grove loop for longer outing.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-21K FLEXIBLE] Central trail running hub. Connects to: Truckee River Legacy Trail (8K out-and-back), Tahoe City to Squaw Valley bike path (16K one way), Burton Creek trails (5K-15K loop options). Paved and dirt mix. Flat to rolling. Multiple bailout points. Popular morning running destination.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 10K-42K+ TECHNICAL] PCT access point for serious trail runners. Options: 10K loop on old logging roads, 21K lollipop on PCT to Watson Lake, 42K+ point-to-point to Donner Summit. Technical singletrack, significant elevation. Training ground for ultra runners. Views of Lake Tahoe. Bring water—limited sources.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-15K LOOP OPTIONS] High-altitude trail running at 8,300 ft. TRT connectors, fire road loops, meadow trails. Popular 5K interval loops, 10K figure-8, 15K TRT out-and-back. Soft singletrack. Alpine flowers July-Aug. Morning best—afternoon thunderstorms common. Good for VO2 max training.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-25K GROOMED] Resort-maintained trail network for summer use. 20+ miles of marked trails. 5K time-trial loop, 10K perimeter, 21K full network. Mix of singletrack and fire road. Maps at village. Water stops at resort base. Good for tempo runs. Chairlift access for downhill-only options.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-30K+ POINT-TO-POINT] FLume Trail north access point. World-famous lake-view running. 5K preview loop, 10K to Marlette Lake, 21K to Tunnel Creek, 30K+ point-to-point to Incline Village. High elevation 7,000-8,500 ft. Exposed—early start essential. Bucket-list Sierra running destination. Bring electrolytes.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-20K HILL TRAINING] Old powerline service roads converted to trails. Consistent 5-8% grades—perfect hill training. 5K repeats, 10K out-and-back, 20K point-to-point to Christmas Valley. Views of Freel Peak. Less crowded than lakeside trails. Good for winter training when high trails snowed in. Self-sufficient required.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 8K-25K ALPINE] Glen Alpine Trail access for mountain runners. 8K to Glen Alpine Falls, 16K lollipop to Susie Lake, 25K+ to Gilmore Lake/Desolation Wilderness boundary. 6,300-8,500 ft elevation. Technical rocky sections. Cascade views, alpine lakes. Bear country awareness. Popular with ultra training groups.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-42K+ HIGH ALPINE] World-class high-altitude running at 8,600 ft. PCT/JMT corridor access. 5K meadow loop, 10K to Soda Springs, 21K to Lyell Canyon bridge, 42K+ to Donohue Pass. Gentle grades, perfect tread. Must-do for any serious mountain runner. Acclimatization essential—don't sprint first day. Afternoon thunderstorms rule.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 10K-30K TECHNICAL ALPINE] 8,150 ft alpine lake trailhead. Connects to Clouds Rest trail, Sunrise Lakes, Half Dome approach. 10K Sunrise Lakes loop, 16K to Clouds Rest junction, 30K+ Half Dome summit return. Technical rocky sections. Dramatic high country. Snow patches into July. Serious mountain running requiring preparation.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-42K SKI RESORT TRAILS] Summer use of ski resort trail network. 5K switchback loops, 10K to Panorama Dome, 21K to Reds Meadow, 42K point-to-point to Devils Postpile. Marked summer trails. 7,800-11,000 ft. Chairlift up/run down options. Mammoth Trail Festival race course. Excellent training at altitude. Café/amenities at lodge.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-15K TOWN CONNECTOR] Town-based trail network connecting to forest service trails. 5K paved loop through town, 8K to Old Mammoth Rd trails, 15K to Lakes Basin path. Flat to gentle grades. Good for: recovery runs, tempo work, evening runs. Lit sections in town. Safe solo running. Connects to Mammoth Creek trail system.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-30K+ ALPINE LAKES] 7 alpine lakes trail network. 5K Lake Mary loop, 10K Mary+George, 18K Duck Pass, 30K+ to PCT junction. 8,900-10,500 ft. Granite and aspen. Water sources available. Multi-loop options. Trail running paradise. Active bear population—follow food storage rules. Afternoon T-storms July-Aug.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 10K-42K+ HIGH SIERRA] Gateway to John Muir Trail section. 10K to Chocolate Lakes, 22K to Bishop Pass, 42K+ to Dusy Basin/JMT junction. 7,800-11,972 ft. Epic Sierra high country. Alpine lakes, glaciers, 13,000 ft peaks. Must-do for experienced mountain runners. Acclimatize at lower elevations first. Weather changes fast.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-15K HILL INTERVALS] Desert bouldering area service roads and singletrack. 5K rolling warm-up, 10K out-and-back to Sagehen Meadows, 15K loop via old mining roads. 5,200-6,200 ft. Exposed—morning/evening best. Views of Owens Valley, White Mountains. Good for heat training. Less crowded than creek canyon trails. Watch for snakes in summer.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-21K VALLEY FLOOR] Paved bike path and dirt trail combo around valley floor. 5K east valley, 10K full loop, 21K combined with Mirror Lake extension. 4,000 ft elevation. Flat to gentle grades. Iconic views throughout. Start 5am to avoid crowds. Run beneath El Cap, past Yosemite Falls. Bucket list even for non-runners. Crowded mid-day.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 10K-30K HIGH ELEVATION] 7,000-7,500 ft running along paved road (closed to cars in AM before 9am or after 4pm). Connects Sentinel Dome, Taft Point, McGurk Meadow, Bridalveil Creek trails. 10K to Sentinel Dome and back, 16K to Ostrander Lake trailhead, 30K+ point-to-point to Wawona. High Sierra meadows, sequoia groves. Less crowded than valley.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-12K MELLOW] Historic 4-mile loop around Wawona Meadow. Gentle, rolling, forested. Historic hotel views. 5K out-and-back on meadow edge, 6.4K full loop, 12K combined with Swinging Bridge. 4,000 ft elevation. Accessible year-round (snow possible Dec-Mar). Quiet alternative to valley crowds. Good for recovery runs.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-20K FOREST MEADOWS] 6,500 ft forest trail network. 5K General Grant loop, 10K Bobcat Point trail, 20K connector to Redwood Mountain Grove. Sequoia forest running—unique experience. Gentle grades, soft duff trails. Good for tempo runs. Bear frequent—stay alert. Seasonal road closure possible winter.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-25K CANYON RUNNING] 4,600 ft canyon floor running along South Fork Kings River. 5K Zumwalt Meadow loop, 10K Roads End trail, 16K Mist Falls, 25K+ to Paradise Valley. Canyon walls 4,000 ft overhead. River crossings (easy). Dramatic scenery unique to this zone. Hot midday—early morning best. Limited shade—bring water.

[TRAIL RUNNING - 5K-15K SEQUOIA FOREST] 6,500 ft trail running among world's largest trees. 5K Big Trees Trail, 8K Crescent Meadow loop, 15K Moro Rock connector. Surreal running experience—giants overhead. Mix of paved, dirt, boardwalk. Combine multiple trails for marathon training. Less crowded than Sherman Tree area. Must-stop: Parker Group sequoias.
Flat stroll to carbonated Soda Springs and historic Parsons Memorial Lodge. Where Muir and Johnson conceived Yosemite NP. Unpaved, well-maintained.
NPS top family pick: kids love touching carbonated spring water. Mostly flat, stroller-accessible. Bring sunscreen at 8,600 ft.
Short walk then easy granite scramble. First panoramic view of Tuolumne Meadows from summit. Slippery when wet.
NPS-listed short family option. Easy scramble for kids 5+. Granite friction — closed-toe shoes recommended.
Low-angle exfoliation dome — beginner granite friction. Potholes (glacial melt features) at base. Good family intro to Tuolumne granite. No permit needed.
Flat JMT along Lyell Fork to Twin Bridges — scenic wading spot. Mostly flat, uneven terrain. Excellent early-morning light on river.
Turn-around version for families. Easy wading access at multiple points. Marmots and deer frequently seen.
One of the finest high-altitude trail runs in the Sierra. Flat runnable JMT/PCT corridor to Lyell Base Camp (~9.7 km one-way). Elevation 8,700–9,500 ft. Wilderness permit for overnight; day use free.
Informal trail circling Tenaya Lake at 8,150 ft. Swimming, paddleboarding, kayaking from shore. No permit required.
Flat shoreline walk with sandy beaches — one of Yosemite's best family swimming spots. Water cold even in July. Accessible picnic tables.
Point-to-point trail run, Tenaya Lake east to Tuolumne Meadows. Mix of use trails and Tioga Rd shoulder. Elevation 8,150–8,600 ft. Car shuttle or park shuttle needed for one-way.
Short informal walk across glacier-polished granite to viewpoint. Half Dome visible. Glacial erratics scattered across slabs. No maintained trail.
Kids enjoy exploring glacial erratic boulders. Educational NPS geology signage on site. Good photo stop.
Glacier-polished slabs and large erratics offer informal scrambling immediately from parking. Natural beginner granite environment. No established problems. NPS geology interpretation on site.

Glen Aulin trail run: JMT/PCT singletrack follows Tuolumne River 8.9 km to Glen Aulin HSC and back. Net downhill going (240 m gain on return). Passes Tuolumne Falls and White Cascade. Elevation 8,600–7,800 ft.
Young Lakes Loop trail run via Glen Aulin or Dog Lake. NPS lists as 13.6-mile day hike. Classic trail running objective reaching Young Lakes at 10,000 ft.
Classic NPS hike: 'Follow the Tuolumne River 5.5 miles as it drops to Glen Aulin.' Passes Tuolumne Falls and White Cascade. Glen Aulin HSC at end. Optional extension to Waterwheel Falls (+3 miles).

Lembert Dome summit hike: 3.8-mile round trip via Dog Lake trail with steep final approach on open granite to summit at 9,450 ft. Panoramic views of meadows and peaks. NPS: 'Stay off domes during thunderstorms.'

Lembert Dome base bouldering on world-class Tuolumne granite. NPS: 'pinching crystals on sun-drenched Tuolumne Meadows domes.' Slab climbing and established problems. Helmets recommended. No permit needed.

Informal meadow loop combining meadow paths, Tuolumne River corridor, Soda Springs, Parsons Lodge. Nearly flat at 8,600 ft. Popular with lodge guests. Mix of dirt trail and grass — muddy early season.
Yosemite Half Marathon: annual road/trail event utilizing Tioga Rd and Tuolumne Meadows. High altitude racing at 8,600 ft. Verify 2026 date and NPS permit status at recreation.gov.

Cathedral Lakes: NPS 'most popular trail climbs steadily to Upper Cathedral Lake.' Lower at 9,288 ft, Upper at 9,585 ft beneath Cathedral Peak (10,911 ft). Wildflowers peak late July. Wilderness permit for overnight.

Elizabeth Lake: 'Glacier-carved lake at the base of Unicorn Peak' (9,503 ft). Steady climb over first 2 miles. Less crowded than Cathedral Lakes. Optional 1-mile loop around lake. No day use permit needed.

Dog Lake (9,170 ft): shallow, warm-for-Sierra swimming lake. Can be combined with Lembert Dome for 6.1 km loop. Forested trail, less exposed on stormy days. Good on days with afternoon thunderstorm risk.

Vogelsang via Rafferty Creek: long day hike to Vogelsang HSC at 10,330 ft. 570 m gain into high alpine zone. Stunning Clark Range and Cathedral Range views. Wilderness permit for overnight. Classic backpacking route.

Gaylor Lakes: 'Climb steadily to ridge with spectacular views of Dana Meadows.' Middle Gaylor Lake then optional cross-country to Great Sierra Mine ruins. Start at 9,945 ft. Best short high-altitude hike in Yosemite.

Mount Dana (13,061 ft): 2nd highest peak in Yosemite. ~9.7 km RT with 945 m gain, all above 9,945 ft. No maintained trail — cross-country on use trail. Outstanding views of Mono Lake and Sierra crest. Afternoon thunderstorms July–Aug.

Mono Pass: 'Climb steadily for views of Mono Lake and eastern Sierra.' Trail gains 300 m to Mono Pass at 10,604 ft on Yosemite boundary. Historic mining-era cabins at pass. Views east to Mono Lake and Great Basin.

World-renowned high-altitude granite bouldering. Established problems at Fairview Dome base (grades V0–V8), Daff Dome, Stately Pleasure Dome. NPS: 'pinching crystals on sun-drenched Tuolumne domes.' Best season July–Sept. NPS climbing regs apply. No permit for bouldering.

Easy out-and-back or connector segment of the Valley Loop Trail along the Merced River past Swinging Bridge and Sentinel Beach. Mostly level dirt and old pavement. Good views of El Capitan and Cathedral Rocks.

Paved out-and-back service road to Mirror Lake (2 mi / 3.2 km RT). Views of Half Dome reflections (best spring/early summer), Tenaya Canyon, Washington Column. Stroller and wheelchair accessible with assistance on paved section. Lake becomes a meadow in late summer. ~1 hour.

Easy stroll around Happy Isles nature area on the valley floor along the Merced River. The John Muir Trail and Mist Trail both start here. Good for wildlife viewing and nature interpretation. The Happy Isles Art and Nature Center is seasonal.

Flat walk along Valley Loop Trail through El Capitan Meadow with unobstructed views of El Capitan (3,000 ft granite face). Excellent spot to watch climbers on El Cap with binoculars. Mostly level trail on dirt/old pavement. Leashed pets prohibited on Valley Loop Trail.

NPS most-visited trail in Yosemite. 1 mi (1.6 km) loop to base of 320-ft Lower Yosemite Fall. Eastern portion is wheelchair accessible. Strollers permitted. Drinking fountain and restrooms along trail. Rocks are slippery even when dry — stay on path. Open year-round; often dry late July–October. ~30 min.

Short 0.5 mi (0.8 km) RT paved trail to base of 620-ft Bridalveil Fall, which flows year-round. Western trail section is wheelchair accessible. Leashed pets allowed. Peak flow and spray in spring/early summer; icy in winter. Stay on paved trail — rocks above viewing platform are slippery. ~20 min.

Completely flat paved loop perfect for young children, strollers, and wheelchairs (when not snow-covered). Views of Yosemite Falls, Half Dome, Glacier Point, and Sentinel Rock. Bicycles and leashed pets allowed. Boardwalks can be slippery when wet. Restrooms and water at trailhead. ~30 min.

Full Mirror Lake loop (5 mi / 8 km, 2-3 hrs) or easy 2 mi RT to the lake on paved road. Paved first mile is stroller and wheelchair accessible (with assistance). Views of Half Dome. Ecology and cultural history exhibits. No water on trail — bring your own. Vault toilets at 0.5 mi and 1 mi. Lake may be dry meadow in late summer.

1.6 mi (2.6 km) RT to Vernal Fall Footbridge via paved trail from Happy Isles (Mist Trail). First paved mile is the busiest trail segment in Yosemite. Views of 317-ft Vernal Fall. Water available at shuttle stop #16 and footbridge (May–Oct). Pets, bicycles, and strollers prohibited beyond Happy Isles. ~1–1.5 hrs.

Full 11.5 mi (18.5 km) loop run around Yosemite Valley on mixed terrain: dirt, rocks, sand, old pavement. Mostly level with some hilly sections. Passes El Capitan, Bridalveil Fall, Swinging Bridge, Sentinel Beach, and Valley views. Pets, bicycles, and strollers prohibited on trail. No water along route — carry your own. 5–7 hr hike; faster for runners.
Half Valley Loop run (7.2 mi / 11.6 km). Good morning trail run option on the less-crowded western valley section. Mixed terrain. 2.5–3.5 hr hiking pace.

JMT segment from Happy Isles to top of Nevada Fall (5.4 mi / 8.7 km one-way via Nevada Fall route). Alternative to the steeper Mist Trail. Rocky trail run with significant elevation gain. Passes Vernal Fall (317 ft) and Nevada Fall (594 ft). No pets or strollers. Water at shuttle stop #16 and footbridge (May–Oct only). Carry 3–4 quarts.

Organized marathon event using Yosemite Valley roads and trails. Route utilizes Valley Loop Trail and Valley roads. Permit-based event run on mostly flat-to-rolling terrain through the valley floor. Check NPS special use permit events calendar for scheduled dates.
Half marathon distance option. Valley floor route on roads and Valley Loop Trail. Flat to gently rolling. Event-based only.

Trail run up Mist Trail from Happy Isles to top of Vernal Fall (2.4 mi / 3.9 km RT). Over 600 granite steps. Significant spray in spring/early summer — trail is slick. Common for sprained ankles and knee injuries. No pets, bicycles, or strollers. Carry 2 quarts water.

Top of Vernal Fall via Mist Trail: 2.4 mi (3.9 km) RT, 1,000 ft (300 m) gain. Over 600 granite steps. Significant mist/spray in spring. Strenuous. 3 hrs. Carry 2 quarts water. No pets, strollers, or bikes.

Top of Nevada Fall via Mist Trail: 5.4 mi (8.7 km) RT, 2,000 ft (610 m) gain. Nevada Fall drops 594 ft. Strenuous. 5–6 hrs. Carry 3–4 quarts water. JMT offers alternate longer route (~4 mi one-way). Swimming illegal at Emerald Pool and Silver Apron.

7.2 mi (11.6 km) RT to top of Yosemite Falls (2,425 ft / 739 m above valley floor). Historic trail built 1873–1877. Strenuous switchback ascent through oak woodland. 6–8 hrs RT. Views of Half Dome, Sentinel Rock, Yosemite Valley. No water on trail — carry 4 quarts. Steep upper section with exposure and loose footing. Can extend to Yosemite Point or Eagle Peak.

4.8 mi (7.7 km) one-way; 9.6 mi (15.5 km) RT. 3,200 ft (975 m) gain to Glacier Point. Continuous steep grade following historic 1872 toll trail route. Views of El Capitan, Yosemite Falls, Half Dome, and Sierra peaks. Union Point viewpoint ~2/3 up trail. Many drop-offs and ledges — not for inexperienced hikers. No water on trail; carry 2 qts one-way. Opens ~May; closes below Union Point after significant snow (Nov–Dec).

2.3 mi (3.7 km) RT from Glacier Point Road trailhead to summit of Sentinel Dome (8,122 ft). Moderate. 360° panoramic views of Yosemite Valley, Half Dome, El Capitan, Yosemite Falls. Final 0.3 mi is a scramble on bare granite dome. No shade; bring sun protection. Can be combined with Taft Point for a 5 mi loop. Glacier Point Road typically open late May–November.

9.4 mi (15.1 km) RT from valley floor to valley rim via dozens of switchbacks. 2,700 ft (820 m) gain. Views of Half Dome, Quarter Domes, Clouds Rest. Less crowded than other rim hikes. Exposed with little shade; rattlesnakes present. Loose sand and rocky terrain — ankle injuries common. 6–7 hrs. No water on trail. Popular winter snowshoe/ski route.

Camp 4 is listed on the National Register of Historic Places for its seminal role in the development of rock climbing as a sport. The boulders immediately around Camp 4 are world-famous, featuring problems ranging V0 to V14 on Yosemite granite. Columbia Boulder and the Camp 4 boulders are the primary attractions. Follow established approach trails. Minimize chalk use; clean holds after use per NPS bouldering regulations. Consider environmental impact before deploying crash pads. No permit required for day-use bouldering.

Columbia Boulder is one of the most historically significant boulders in Yosemite and American climbing history. Located immediately adjacent to Camp 4. Features classic problems on high-quality Yosemite granite. Wide range of difficulty. NPS regulations require following established approach trails, minimizing chalk, and cleaning holds after use. Crash pad use should consider impact on fragile vegetation.

Beginner-friendly circuit of V0–V3 problems on boulders in and around Camp 4. Good introductory Yosemite granite bouldering. Some of the best bouldering in Yosemite per NPS climbing page. NPS regulations: do not remove lichen or vegetation to establish problems; remove all trash including tape wrappers; minimize chalk; use established approach trails.

Advanced circuit targeting V8–V14 problems across Camp 4 and Columbia Boulder area. Elite granite bouldering with historic problems. Sessions may span multiple days. Follow NPS Leave No Trace bouldering guidelines. Carry out all waste. Assess environmental impact before placing crash pads on fragile vegetation.

Scattered bouldering areas in the lower/western Yosemite Valley, away from Camp 4. Less crowded than Camp 4 circuits. Yosemite Valley offers some of the best bouldering in the park per NPS. Access via established trails. Per NPS climbing regulations: minimize chalk, clean holds when finished, do not alter rock or vegetation to establish problems, consider crash pad impact on vegetation.

Flat, forested loop around Horseshoe Lake at 8,928 ft. Sandy pumice soil, good fishing access, picnic areas. Part of Inyo National Forest Mammoth Lakes Basin. Tree-kill area caused by volcanic CO2 gas is visible and interpretive signs explain the phenomenon.

Gentle walk along Twin Lakes shoreline through mixed conifer forest at 8,600 ft. Views of Mammoth Crest. Connects to Mammoth Lakes trail system. Can be combined with Tamarack Lodge area paths for longer outing.

Paved and compacted-gravel multi-use path connecting Mammoth Village toward Lakes Basin. Shared with cyclists and strollers. Passes through Shady Rest Park and connects to Mammoth Creek Park. Inyo NF manages portions through Lakes Basin corridor. Suitable for all ages and abilities.

Loop through Reds Meadow Valley from Devils Postpile area to Reds Meadow Resort. Passes through meadow, hot springs pool, and forest. Devils Postpile NM / Inyo NF corridor. Hot springs swimming at Reds Meadow pool (fee). Rainbow Falls can be added as an extension.

5-mile round-trip hike from Devils Postpile trailhead to Rainbow Falls, a 101-foot waterfall on the San Joaquin River. Rolling terrain. Bring water as trail can be hot and dry in summer. Managed by NPS Devils Postpile National Monument. Very popular with families; best visited early morning to avoid crowds.

0.8-mile round trip to the base of the Devils Postpile columnar basalt formation; columns reach 60 feet tall. Optional 15-minute uphill scramble adds ~0.5 mile to reach the top for mosaic views. NPS-managed. Excellent geology education opportunity for children.

Gentle path along Lake Mary shoreline at 8,890 ft. Largest lake in Mammoth Lakes Basin. Easy terrain suitable for strollers on the paved sections; gravel near campgrounds. Beautiful views of Mammoth Crest. Inyo NF managed. Fishing, kayak rentals nearby.

Easy 1.4-mile round trip to Minaret Falls, a small cascading waterfall just outside the NM boundary along the San Joaquin River corridor. Flat to gently rolling. Good first hike for young children. Shaded riparian habitat. NPS Devils Postpile NM.

Partially shaded loop around Convict Lake at 7,583 ft with dramatic views of 12,000+ ft peaks including Laurel and Bloody mountains. Rocky talus on north shore section is moderately challenging for small children. Inyo NF managed. Fishing, picnicking, and horse rentals at resort.

Evening single-loop trail race (~8.5 km / 5.3 miles) with ~800 ft elevation gain/loss. Uptown Trail ascent to Party Summit aid station then Downtown Trail descent. Disco lights in trees illuminate the course. Starts at 6:45 PM at Village Plaza. Part of Mammoth Trail Fest weekend in September.

Popular local training route from town up Lake Mary Rd through Mammoth Lakes Basin visiting Twin Lakes, Lake Mary, Horseshoe Lake and back. Combination of paved road, bike path, and dirt trail. High-altitude training at 8,000–9,000 ft. Used by local running community and as prep for Mammoth Trail Fest races.

Three-day stage event combining Dragon's Back Ascent (4.8 km / 2,400 ft gain), Mammoth 50K (51.5 km / 7,000 ft gain), and Mammoth 26K (26 km / 3,900 ft gain) — totaling ~51 miles / 82 km and 13,400+ ft of vertical gain across one weekend in September.

Strenuous 11-mile round trip hike from Sherwin Creek TH to Valentine Lake in the John Muir Wilderness via Inyo NF. Significant elevation gain through open slopes, granite slabs, and meadows. Less crowded than Lakes Basin trails. Wilderness permit required for overnight; good fishing at lake.

8-mile round trip from Convict Lake (7,583 ft) up Convict Creek Canyon into the John Muir Wilderness via Inyo NF. Dramatic granite walls and canyon scenery. Trail follows creek upstream toward Mildred and Dorothy lakes. Can be done as an out-and-back to Lake Genevieve or extended further into the wilderness.

7-mile round trip from Lake George TH to Mammoth Crest (10,400 ft) via Crystal Lake. Exposed ridgeline with sweeping views of Long Valley Caldera, Mono Lake, and the Minarets. Class 2 terrain on upper sections. Inyo National Forest. Best July–September when snow-free.

Scattered volcanic boulders in and around Shady Rest Park / Mammoth Community Park adjacent to Inyo NF. Beginner-friendly area with low-angle problems V0–V3. Very short walk from parking. Town setting; suitable for families with beginners. Best in spring and fall to avoid summer afternoon heat.

Volcanic rhyolite boulders around the Earthquake Fault geological site on Inyo NF land near Mammoth Mountain. The fault itself (a fissure crack) is the primary attraction but boulders in the vicinity offer V0–V4 problems. Excellent combination with the free geological interpretive walk. Good intro area for beginners.

Volcanic tuff and rhyolite boulders at Deadman Summit on Inyo NF land. Less-visited area with problems V0–V6 on quality volcanic rock. Good extended-season option (often climbable April–November) due to lower elevation. Roadside access makes this a quick stop for climbers passing on US-395.

Granite boulders in the Lake George basin at ~9,000 ft on Inyo NF land. Problems range V1–V7 on clean Sierra granite. Short approach. Often combined with hiking Crystal Lake or Mammoth Crest. Season typically July–October due to snowpack at this elevation. Stunning alpine setting below Crystal Crag.
Sand Harbor to Memorial Point Trail (0.5 mi one-way) plus Sand Point Nature Trail (0.33 mi). Flat, paved/packed-gravel shoreline. Crystal-clear water views. ADA-accessible sections.
Sand Point Nature Trail (0.33 mi) is fully handicap-accessible with interpretive signs and lake views. Perfect for all ages and abilities.
