3-mile paved multi-use bikeway from Incline Village south to Sand Harbor along Nevada's east shore. Flat, scenic lakeside path. Called 'America's Most Beautiful Bikeway'.
Out-and-back run on 3-mile paved East Shore Trail from Incline Village to Sand Harbor. Gentle grade, stunning lake views, minimal traffic.
6 miles of unpaved fire roads through coniferous forest on south-facing slopes of the north shore Tahoe Basin. South-facing slopes, seasonal wildflowers in spring, fall cottonwood color.
Gentle fire road loops on northeast side of Tahoe City. Wildlife and bird watching. No traffic. Dogs allowed on fire roads (seasonal ski trail restrictions apply in winter).
Undeveloped fire road network near Tahoe City. Popular for trail running on packed dirt/gravel surfaces. Cross-country ski trails double as running routes in summer.
Nearly 2 miles of Lake Tahoe frontage along the west shore. Flat lakeside walk past Hellman-Ehrman Mansion (Pine Lodge), pier, and nature center. Over 20km of marked trails in park.
Flat shoreline path with swimming beach, pier, and nature center with bird and wildlife exhibits. Hellman-Ehrman Mansion tour available. Excellent for children.

979 feet of sandy beach frontage on the north shore. Flat lakeside stroll along sandy beach. Playground, basketball half-court, 18 picnic tables under Jeffrey pines. Dogs on leash on sidewalks/picnic areas (not beach).

Flat lakeside walking along Tahoe City's Commons Beach and adjacent bike path to downtown. Beach access, lake views, picnic areas. Bike path leads into downtown Tahoe City. Best combined with Truckee River Legacy Trail.

Tahoe City (64 Acres) to Brockway Summit segment: 20.5 miles one-way. Rugged lava cliffs, cinder cones, Watson Lake (treat water), Truckee River Canyon views. One of the most scenic TRT segments. Suitable for experienced trail runners.
Day hike out-and-back on TRT from 64 Acres toward Watson Lake (~5 miles one-way, 610m gain). Lava formations, cinder cones, forest, lake views. Watson Lake a scenic turnaround point.
Brockway Summit to Mt. Rose Summit / Tahoe Meadows: 20.2 miles one-way. Highest point on TRT is Relay Peak at 10,338 ft. Tremendous panoramic views. Requires shuttle or point-to-point logistics.
Donner Lake Rim Trail full loop (~18.6 miles). Circumnavigates Donner Lake with views of the Sierra. Mix of singletrack and fire road. ~3,800 ft gain full loop. Popular trail run and race course.
Out-and-back day hike on Donner Lake Rim Trail (~6 miles). Climb above Donner Lake for panoramic views of the lake and surrounding Sierra peaks. Trails range from moderate to strenuous.

Barker Pass to Tahoe City (64 Acres): 16.4 miles one-way. Abundant wildlife, creeks, wildflowers, Page Meadow. Mix of forested ridgeline and open meadow running. Popular TRT segment for point-to-point runs with shuttle.

Western States 100 Mile Endurance Run. Starts at 6,200 ft elevation, climbs 2,550 ft to Emigrant Pass (8,750 ft) in first 4.5 miles. Total ~18,090 ft gain / ~22,970 ft descent to Auburn, CA. One of the world's most prestigious ultramarathons. Annual event last full weekend of June.

5-mile round-trip into Granite Chief Wilderness. Gains ~1,400 ft to a cluster of 5 alpine lakes at ~7,400 ft. Well-maintained trail with switchbacks through pine forest and granite. Swim in summer. Popular but rewarding.
Short 2-mile round-trip hike to Eagle Rock summit. ~400 ft gain. Outstanding panoramic views of Lake Tahoe and north shore. One of the best view hikes for effort in the area. Good for older children.
7-mile round-trip to Castle Peak summit (9,103 ft). ~1,850 ft total gain. Passes Castle Valley, sub-peak, and exposed rocky summit with 360-degree views of Donner Summit area and Sierra crest.
4-mile round-trip to Donner Peak summit (8,019 ft) via PCT. ~1,250 ft gain. Excellent views of Donner Lake and Truckee basin. Rocky exposed terrain near summit. Short but steep final push.

9-mile round-trip day hike toward Twin Peaks (8,878 ft) via TRT from Ward Creek area. Dense forest transitions to sub-alpine terrain. ~3,000 ft gain. Solitary, undeveloped trails through Ward Creek's upland forest and meadowland.

Donner Summit is one of the premier bouldering destinations in the Sierra Nevada, featuring hundreds of problems on high-quality granite. Problems range from V0 to V13+. Short approach from road. The area encompasses multiple boulder fields including the School Rock and Iron Door areas. Best season May–October. Elevation ~7,000 ft.

School Rock at Donner Summit features approachable beginner-to-intermediate granite bouldering problems. V0–V5 concentration. Clean Sierra granite with excellent friction. 5–10 minute approach from parking. Ideal for learning bouldering technique in alpine setting.

Granite outcroppings and slabs in the Sugar Pine Point / General Creek drainage area. Lower-angle granite features with juggy holds. Good for beginners and intermediates. Part of the broader west shore Tahoe granite band. Less developed than Donner Summit but scenic lakeside setting.
Flat to gently rolling rim walk above Owens River Gorge on BLM Volcanic Tablelands. Views into the 150-ft basalt gorge. Informal trail on open terrain; stay back from rim edges. No fee.

Paved and maintained gravel path around much of the Lake Sabrina shoreline at 9,128 ft. Outstanding Sierra scenery; fishing access. Mostly flat with minor undulation along the east shore. Family and dog friendly.

Gentle loop around the west and south shore of South Lake at 9,755 ft elevation. Trout fishing access; stunning views of Mt. Goode and Hurd Peak. Trail is partly on maintained path, partly informal lakeshore. Short and accessible for all fitness levels.
Riparian walk along Bishop Creek from the south edge of Bishop toward lower canyon. Cottonwood and willow shade; bird watching. Informal but well-worn path. Connects to city trail network.
Easy loop around Rock Creek Lake at 10,850 ft. Views of Wheeler Crest and Little Lakes Valley peaks. Popular fishing destination; paved and dirt paths. Accessible for families with young children.

Flat paved loop through Bishop City Park along the Bishop Creek channel. Playground, picnic shelters, skate park, and open lawn adjacent. Stroller and wheelchair accessible. Ideal for toddlers and young children.

Short family-oriented walk from the Lake Sabrina parking area to the picnic sites and boat launch along the lake's east shore. Flat and easy; suitable for ages 3+. Great mountain views and fishing. At 9,128 ft — acclimatize if coming from low elevation.

Walk from North Lake parking along the inlet stream and aspen groves toward North Lake shoreline. Aspen color peak in October. Gentle grade; suitable for ages 5+. Out-and-back. Great introduction to the high Sierra for kids. Connects to Piute Pass trailhead.

Gradual walk from Rock Creek Lake TH up into Little Lakes Valley. Passes multiple alpine lakes (Marsh, Heart, Box). Incredible scenery with Mt. Morgan and Bear Creek Spire. Trail is well-maintained and easy to follow. Suitable for ages 6+. Out-and-back to first lake ~2.4 km.

Easy walk on BLM open land in Owens Valley south of Bishop. Views of Sierra Nevada and White Mountains. Desert terrain with sage. Good for young children learning to hike on dirt. No maintained trail; open cross-country on gentle terrain.

Annual trail running event using Buttermilk Road and surrounding BLM terrain west of Bishop. Rolling high-desert terrain with views of the Sierra crest. Multiple distance options typically offered (5K, 10K, half marathon). Also a popular training route year-round. Event organized by local running clubs.

Out-and-back run up paved Rock Creek Road from Tom's Place (6,998 ft) to Rock Creek Lake (10,850 ft). Stunning canyon scenery. Moderate grade (~4%) suitable for strong recreational runners. Light vehicle traffic. Road closed to cars in winter — excellent snow-free running May through November.

Out-and-back run on South Lake Road from lower canyon to South Lake (9,755 ft). Passes multiple USFS campgrounds and pullouts. Road winds through Bishop Creek Canyon with views of granite peaks. Excellent training run for high-altitude adaptation.

Loop run on BLM dirt roads and open terrain across the Volcanic Tablelands north of Bishop. Flat to gently rolling basalt terrain. Early morning runs offer outstanding views of the Sierra Nevada. Year-round access. Good for base-building before high-altitude runs.

Piute Pass (11,423 ft) from North Lake TH through John Muir Wilderness. Passes Loch Leven and Piute Lake before the final switchbacks to the pass. Exceptional fall color in aspens below 10,000 ft (September–October). Well-maintained trail. Views of Humphreys Basin from the pass.

Out-and-back hike through Little Lakes Valley to Ruby Lake (11,121 ft) in the John Muir Wilderness. Passes Marsh Lake, Heart Lake, Box Lake, Long Lake, and Chickenfoot Lake. One of the most scenic hikes in the Eastern Sierra. Trail is well-maintained. Route continues to Mono Pass for additional mileage.

Classic Eastern Sierra hike to Kearsarge Pass (11,760 ft) from Onion Valley. Passes Gilbert, Flower, and Heart lakes before the rocky switchbacks to the pass. Views into Kings Canyon NP and the Kearsarge Pinnacles from the top. Trail is well-maintained and heavily used as a JMT entry point. About 45 min south of Bishop via US-395 and Onion Valley Rd.

World-class granite bouldering on rounded glacial erratics at 4,700–5,200 ft. Problems range V0–V15. Home to legendary lines including The Mandala (V12), Evilution (V12), and Grandma Peabody (V10). The Druids, Peabody, and High Boulders sectors are the main zones. Best season October–April. Very hot in summer. Magnetic rock; bring more chalk than usual.

Granite bouldering in lower Pine Creek Canyon on Inyo NF land. Problems range V0–V8. Less developed than Buttermilks or Volcanic Tablelands; fewer crowds and a more exploratory feel. Access via lower Pine Creek Road before the pack station. Best combined with a hike up the canyon.

Bouldering on the northern end of the Volcanic Tablelands, BLM land north of Bishop. Problems range V0–V7 on featured tuff. Less documented than Happy/Sad drainages; good for beginner to intermediate climbers seeking solitude. Fish Slough is also a wildlife area — be aware of sensitive habitat zones. Year-round access; best October–April.

NPS most-visited trail. Paved 2-mile loop beginning near General Sherman Tree. Passes dozens of named giant sequoias including President Tree, Chief Sequoyah, and Senate/House groups. Some gentle grades and stairs. World's largest trees by volume.

Short paved 0.33-mile loop to General Grant Tree — the Nation's Christmas Tree (proclaimed by President Coolidge, 1926) and world's third-largest tree by volume. Also passes Gamlin Cabin and the Fallen Monarch, a massive hollow log.

Very short paved accessible walk to a powerful waterfall rushing through a narrow granite chute. Shaded, cool, and dramatic. One of the easiest highlights in Cedar Grove. Trail is paved and relatively accessible per NPS.

Paved 0.75-mile accessible loop circling Round Meadow near Giant Forest Museum. Trailside panels describe giant sequoia ecology. Flat, wheelchair accessible, ~1 hour round trip. One of the best introductory sequoia experiences.
Lightly traveled 1.5-mile loop through meadows, creeks, mixed conifer and sequoia forest near Grant Grove. Quieter alternative to the General Grant Tree Trail. Gentle terrain suitable for most ages.

0.5-mile paved path descending from Wolverton Rd to the world's largest tree by volume (General Sherman Tree, 274.9 ft tall, 36.5 ft diameter at base). Path includes some stairs; accessible alternative from Generals Hwy is flat and paved. Return is uphill. Strollers manageable on accessible route.

Giant Forest Museum plus Big Trees Trail (paved, accessible 0.75-mile meadow loop). Museum has interactive exhibits on sequoia ecology. Beetle Rock — a large granite outcrop behind the museum — is an easy short walk for wide views. Perfect for families with young children.

Flat, 1.6-mile loop through meadow and forest alongside Kings River, framed by 2,000-ft granite canyon walls. Crosses swinging bridge. Sandy unpaved sections; can flood in spring. Excellent wildlife and wildflower viewing. One of the best easy walks in Kings Canyon.

2-mile loop through remnants of a late-1800s logged giant sequoia grove. Massive stumps and second-growth trees; interpretive history of logging era. Meadow for wildlife viewing. Good intro hike for kids, with interesting historical context.

Very short flat walk to Knapp's Cabin, a historic 1920s stone fishing cabin used by George Knapp for fishing expeditions. Easy riverside stroll suitable for all ages. Combine with river viewpoints nearby.

Road run on Generals Highway from Ash Mountain Entrance (1,700 ft) up to Giant Forest Museum (~6,400 ft). Winding mountain road with vehicle traffic; run facing traffic and use caution. No dedicated lane. Spectacular canyon and sequoia scenery. Popular car-to-car shuttle run. Approximately 18 miles one-way.

Road run from Grant Grove (6,500 ft) descending Kings Canyon on Hwy 180 through one of the deepest canyons in North America to Cedar Grove (4,600 ft). Net descent but rolling terrain. Vehicle traffic; run facing traffic. One of the most scenic road runs in California. 30 miles one-way.

1.7-mile (one-way) easy trail run along Marble Fork of Kaweah River to Tokopah Falls. Wide, well-maintained trail with minimal obstacles. Good introduction to trail running in Sequoia. Return same route for ~3.4-mile run. Best in late spring/summer.

8-mile round-trip trail run to Mist Falls, one of the largest waterfalls in Sequoia and Kings Canyon. First ~3 miles relatively flat along South Fork Kings River through forest and chaparral with impressive rapids. Final mile gains 600 ft to falls. Sandy trail; some rocky sections.

Annual half marathon event run on Generals Highway through lower Sequoia National Park. Course climbs from Three Rivers foothills into the sequoia groves. Stunning scenery; strenuous due to elevation gain. Typically held in spring. Check Sequoia Parks Conservancy or local race directors for current event status.

4.4-mile round-trip run to Big Baldy summit (8,209 ft) with 600-ft gain. Well-defined trail on granite ridge with views of Redwood Canyon, Buck Rock Fire Tower, and Sierra. Good moderate trail run with non-technical terrain.

Strenuous 14-mile round trip from Wolverton (6,800 ft) to Alta Peak summit (11,204 ft). Gains ~4,800 ft total. Long day hike through forests, meadows, and exposed granite. Panoramic 360° views including Mt. Whitney on clear days. Start early; bring layers. Snow possible into July. One of Sequoia's premier summit hikes.

13-mile loop from Wolverton through Heather, Aster, Emerald, and Pear Lakes (loop option via Watchtower). Alpine lake scenery above treeline. Watchtower route includes a knife-edge ridge with dramatic views. Can be done as long day hike. Snow may block trail into July. Wildflowers peak in July–August.

5-mile round trip gaining 1,200 ft via Hotel Creek Trail on south-facing slope. Switchbacks up through chaparral to forested ridge and rocky outcrop with gorgeous panoramic view up and down Kings Canyon. Allow 3-4 hours. Best done in cooler morning hours. Goes through 2024 Happy Fire burn area.

8-mile round-trip loop (or 1.25 miles beyond Cedar Grove Overlook) joining Lewis Creek Trail through a burn area from 1980 and the 2024 Happy Fire. Last 2 miles follows trail above roadway. Strenuous; allow ~5 hours. Excellent canyon and backcountry views.

8-mile round-trip moderate hike to Mist Falls, one of the largest waterfalls in Sequoia and Kings Canyon. Sandy trail follows glaciated South Fork Canyon past impressive rapids and cascades. First part relatively flat; final mile gains 600 ft. Allow 4-5 hours. Good gateway hike for Rae Lakes Loop.

8.4-mile round trip (4.2 mi one-way) to Monarch Lakes from Sawtooth trailhead at 7,800 ft. Traverses meadows, red fir forest, and avalanche-scoured Chihuahua Bowl with views of Timber Gap and Great Western Divide. Footing very loose on upper section. Early start recommended; west-facing slope.

NPS lists Buena Vista Peak (8,209 ft, 2-mile round trip) as one of the easiest rock climbing/bouldering approaches in the parks. Granite dome with scattered boulders and low-angle slabs. 360° views of sequoias in Redwood Canyon and Sierra Nevada. Best for beginner-to-intermediate bouldering. No technical gear required for basic scrambling.

NPS identifies Moro Rock west face as 1,000 vertical feet of cracks and knobs — one of the easiest climbing sites in the park. Lower granite slabs around the base offer bouldering. Approach via short trail to base. Seasonal closure during peregrine falcon nesting (summer). No power drills or permanent fixed anchors without special use permit.

Hospital Rock is a large granite boulder complex in the Foothills zone (~2,700 ft elevation) with documented bouldering on the main rock and surrounding granite outcrops. Area also features Monache (Yokuts) Native American pictographs. Year-round access (lower elevation). Rattlesnakes, poison oak, and heat hazards in summer. Popular with local climbers as a warm-weather alternative when higher elevations are snowbound.

NPS identifies Tokopah Domes near Lodgepole as a climbing area. Granite domes and slabs above the Tokopah Valley offer multi-pitch trad climbing and bouldering opportunities. Access via Tokopah Falls trail then cross-country to dome bases. Elevation ~7,000 ft. No power drills; leave-no-trace principles required.

NPS identifies the Bubbs Creek area (8-mile hike from Road's End) as offering multi-pitch crack climbing near Charlito Dome and Charlotte Dome. Remote granite bouldering and crack climbing at elevation. Best for experienced climbers. No motorized equipment or permanent fixed anchors without permit. Access road open May–November.

NPS lists Little Baldy (just over 8,000 ft) as a park climbing area accessible via 1.5-mile trail from Little Baldy Saddle. Granite dome with bouldering on summit slabs and lower flanks. Views of Great Western Divide. Suitable for beginner to intermediate. No technical gear required for basic scrambling.

USFS-designated easy interpretive walk along Taylor Creek to Lake Tahoe. Famous for annual Kokanee salmon spawn (Oct). Boardwalk through meadow and willows. Wheelchair accessible sections. Stream profile chamber lets visitors view fish underwater.

NPS/USFS top family pick for South Lake Tahoe. Salmon viewing in October is an unmissable kid experience. Flat boardwalk trail, stroller accessible. Visitor Center has hands-on exhibits. Restrooms at trailhead.

Walk from Vikingsholm parking area down to Emerald Bay beach and historic Vikingsholm Castle (tours available summer). 160 m descent/ascent. California State Parks-managed. Strenuous for small children on return. One of the most photographed spots in California.

Family walk to Vikingsholm Castle on Emerald Bay — rated one of the finest examples of Scandinavian architecture in North America. Free to walk to; castle tours fee. Beach at bay bottom. Bring water and snacks; no services below overlook.

El Dorado National Forest trail to Cascade Falls — 100+ ft waterfall on Cascade Creek. 1.6 km each way. Rocky terrain, some boulder scrambling near falls. Best flow spring through early July. Spectacular views of Cascade Lake below.

Popular family destination for waterfall viewing. Short enough for older kids (5+). Rocky terrain requires sturdy shoes. Peak flow late spring. Stay back from waterfall edges — rocks are slippery. No restrooms at trailhead.

El Dorado National Forest volcanic rock bouldering and top-rope climbing area near Sierra-at-Tahoe resort. Several distinct spires and boulder fields. Grades range V0–V8. Solitude compared to Lover's Leap. Best season May–October. No permit required.

California State Parks shoreline trail through D.L. Bliss SP along the western shore of Lake Tahoe. Crystal-clear water, granite boulders, and forested slopes. Connects to Vikingsholm/Emerald Bay as part of the Rubicon Trail. Light traffic compared to busier South Shore spots.

Family walk to Calawee Cove — one of Lake Tahoe's best accessible sandy beaches within D.L. Bliss SP. Short walk from parking. Restrooms at beach. Calmer water than open South Shore. Good for small kids.
Taylor Creek Stream Trail: flat loop along Taylor Creek through meadows and aspen groves to Lake Tahoe shoreline. Stream Profile Chamber underwater viewing window mid-route.

Best family walk in the South Shore. Paved/compacted gravel, stroller-accessible much of the way. October kokanee salmon run is a highlight for kids. Stream Profile Chamber provides underwater fish viewing.

Vikingsholm Trail: steep 1-mi descent to Vikingsholm Castle on Emerald Bay shore, return 400 ft gain. Paved path. No rides out – plan for uphill return. Emerald Bay overlook at trailhead is a shorter walk option (0.1 mi, flat).
Walk down to Vikingsholm Castle – a Scandinavian replica on Emerald Bay. Kids enjoy the castle architecture and lake views. Steep return uphill; bring water. No dogs allowed on this trail.

Rubicon Trail from Vikingsholm south toward Eagle Point: scenic foot trail wrapping around Emerald Bay. No dogs. DL Bliss State Park closed 2025 (vehicle/pedestrian); access from Emerald Bay side only. Approximately 2.5 mi one-way to Eagle Point.
Lower Eagle Falls viewpoint: short walk behind the visitor area to view the lower cascade of Eagle Falls. 0.25 mi each way. Paved path to viewpoint.
Eagle Falls Lower Loop: short family-friendly hike from trailhead to Lower Eagle Falls and back. Dogs allowed. Impressive waterfall view; some rocky terrain near falls.
Eagle Lake via Eagle Falls Trail: 3.0 mi round trip from trailhead climbing through granite to alpine Eagle Lake (elev. 7,421 ft). Desolation Wilderness – day permit required. Rocky, strenuous.

Cascade Falls Trail: 1.0 mi one-way to Cascade Falls (over 200 ft tall waterfall). Best in late spring/early summer. Moderate rocky trail with great payoff. Some scrambling near falls. Dog-friendly on leash.
Cascade Falls is a popular family destination – spectacular waterfall 2.0 mi RT. Rocky sections near the end require care with small children. Best May–July when flow is strong.

Mt. Tallac Trail: ~10 mi RT, summit 9,735 ft. Classic South Shore hike via Tallac Lake and the Cross snowfield to summit overlooking Lake Tahoe. 3,000+ ft gain. Desolation Wilderness permit required. Strenuous, exposed summit section.
Mt. Tallac summit run – popular with trail runners. High-altitude exposure above 9,000 ft. Rocky technical terrain on upper section. Best July–September. Permit required.

Glen Alpine to Lake of the Woods / Gilmore Lake: 4–6 mi one-way into Desolation Wilderness. Multiple lake destinations. Wilderness permit required. Granite and pine terrain, alpine lakes. Elevation starts ~6,560 ft.
Glen Alpine Trail to Grass Lake: 3.5 mi RT, easier Desolation Wilderness entry. Excellent for a first wilderness hike. Permit required.
Echo Lakes shoreline walk: flat trail around Lower and Upper Echo Lake. 2.5 mi RT to Upper Echo Lake dam. Stunning granite scenery, no significant elevation. Optional water taxi shortcut.
Echo Lakes to Lake of the Woods via PCT/Desolation Wilderness: 9 mi RT. Classic Desolation Wilderness dayhike through granite basins. Permit required. Water taxi can reduce distance.
Echo Lakes flat run along shoreline: excellent high-altitude (7,400 ft) trail run on relatively flat, packed-dirt trail. Out-and-back to Upper Echo Lake. Stunning alpine scenery.
Echo Lakes shoreline walk to Upper Echo Lake: flat, scenic, kid-friendly. Granite boulders to scramble on. Water taxi option adds excitement for kids. Fee parking.

Fallen Leaf Lake Road walk: flat paved road walk along the west shore of Fallen Leaf Lake. Stunning lake and mountain views. Turn around at campground area or continue further. Excellent for all ages.
Fallen Leaf Lake shoreline walk: paved road and informal trails along the 2-mi-long lake. Popular with families and casual walkers. Views of Mt. Tallac reflected in lake.

Fallen Leaf Lake Road out-and-back run: 6-mi lollipop along paved road and dirt track around the lake, starting from Hwy 89 junction. Popular local run at ~6,400 ft elevation. Mostly flat with minor grades.

Angora Lakes trail from Fallen Leaf Lake Rd: 1.7 mi one-way to lower Angora Lake, continuing to Upper Angora Lake. Steep uphill. Stunning granite cirque. Angora Lakes Resort at upper lake (seasonal refreshments).
Pope Beach to Camp Richardson shoreline walk: flat sandy beach and forest path along Lake Tahoe's south shore. 1.5 mi one-way. Family-friendly, views of Emerald Bay and mountains across the lake.
South Shore Lake Tahoe shoreline run: Pope Beach to Camp Richardson to Kiva Beach and back. ~6 mi RT on flat sandy/packed trail along lake. Beautiful mountain backdrop. Popular morning run.
Camp Richardson beach walk: flat sandy trail from Camp Richardson marina to Pope Beach. Ideal for families – flat, short, beautiful lake and mountain views. Beach swimming in summer.

Ralston Peak Trail: ~8 mi RT to Ralston Peak summit (9,235 ft). Desolation Wilderness entry via Echo Summit trailhead. Sweeping views of Lake Tahoe and Desolation Wilderness. Rocky upper section. Best July–October.
Granite Lake via Bayview Trail: 2.0 mi RT, 900 ft gain in first mile. Short but steep to beautiful alpine Granite Lake in Desolation Wilderness. Wildflowers June–Aug. Permit required.
Bayview Trail to Velma Lakes (7.5 mi RT): Extended Desolation Wilderness route from Bayview through Granite Lake to Velma Lakes chain. Stunning granite alpine terrain. Permit required.

Lake Tahoe Marathon (annual event, typically October): paved/trail course around south Lake Tahoe corridor at ~6,200 ft elevation. Also offers half-marathon (21.1 km) and 10-mi options. Altitude significantly increases difficulty.
Big Meadow Loop (TRT): ~6 mi via TRT through open meadows and conifer forest with wildflower displays. Views of Freel Peak. Moderate terrain on Tahoe Rim Trail. No permit required.
TRT Big Meadow to Round Lake segment run: ~9 mi RT on Tahoe Rim Trail through meadows, aspen groves, and Meiss Meadows. High-altitude trail run at 7,200–8,400 ft. Non-technical, flowy singletrack.
Big Meadow to Big Meadow Creek: short family walk on TRT through open meadow with stream crossing. 2 mi RT, mostly flat. Wildflowers in summer. Easy access off Hwy 89.

World-famous granite crag at 5,720 ft on South Fork American River near Strawberry, El Dorado NF. Primarily sport/trad climbing (5.6–5.12) but excellent bouldering on lower crags and approach slabs. V0–V8. Best season April–November. BLM/USFS access — no permit required. ~45 min from South Lake Tahoe.
Lover's Leap summit trail: hike above the wall to summit plateau at ~6,800 ft for expansive views of American River canyon. 3 mi RT. Steep in sections. Use climbers' trail from parking.

Rubicon Trail (full traverse, D.L. Bliss to Vikingsholm): ~5.5 mi one-way along Lake Tahoe shoreline. One of the most scenic lakeside trails in the Sierra Nevada. No dogs. Park currently closed 2025 – access from Emerald Bay end instead.
Rubicon Trail north section near D.L. Bliss beach area: shorter lake-level walk on one of California's most scenic shoreline trails. Crystal-clear water, granite slabs. No dogs.

Emerald Bay Overlook: 0.1 mi flat walk from Vikingsholm parking to the iconic Emerald Bay viewpoint. One of the most photographed spots in California. Accessible to all fitness levels. Short but spectacular.

Best family photo stop on Lake Tahoe – Emerald Bay overlook with Vikingsholm Castle and Fannette Island below. Nearly flat walk from parking. Combine with Vikingsholm Castle hike for longer excursion.
Heavenly Tamarack Trail (summer): gondola to observation deck at 9,123 ft, then hike along ridgeline trail with 360° views of Lake Tahoe and Nevada. Gondola fee required. Well-marked resort trails.
Heavenly Mountain observation deck walk: take gondola to top, short interpretive walk to observation deck at 9,123 ft. Views stretch to Nevada desert and Lake Tahoe simultaneously.

Granite bouldering on Angora Ridge: scattered granite boulders with V0–V6 problems on high-quality Sierra Nevada granite at ~7,200 ft. Informal/unestablished area. Short approach from Angora Ridge Rd. Beautiful forest setting, views of Fallen Leaf Lake. Best May–Oct.

Horsetail Falls Trail from Pyramid Creek: 3.5 mi RT, ~1,700 ft gain to the base of Horsetail Falls (500 ft waterfall in stages). Very steep rocky scramble to upper falls. Desolation Wilderness permit required above falls. Best June–early August.
Pyramid Creek to Lake of the Woods trail run: ~6 mi RT, technical rocky ascent. Desolation Wilderness permit required. High-altitude (7,000–8,400 ft). Only for experienced trail runners.
Kiva Beach to Pope Beach shoreline walk: ~1.5 mi one-way on flat sandy/forest path along Lake Tahoe south shore. Connects multiple USFS beach areas. Stunning lake views.
South Shore shoreline run: Kiva Beach east toward El Dorado Beach and South Lake Tahoe beach access. ~3–6 mi depending on turnaround. Paved multi-use path and sandy trails. Flat and fast.
Kiva Beach family walk: flat beach and forest loop connecting Kiva Beach picnic area, shoreline, and forest path. Ideal for families with young children. Sandy beach access for wading.

Granite boulders immediately accessible from Hwy 50 pullouts at Echo Summit (7,382 ft). El Dorado National Forest. Grades V0–V5. Great high-elevation bouldering stopover on the way from South Lake Tahoe to Desolation Wilderness. Year-round access when road is clear of snow.

Granite crag at the base of Alpine Meadows Road with established bouldering problems on the lower cliffs. Part of the Tahoe National Forest. Mix of V0–V6 problems on solid Sierra granite. Developed by local Tahoe climbers; best accessed via short scramble from roadside pullout. Often quieter than Donner Summit on weekends. Year-round access when snow-free (May–Oct typical). No permit required.

Pie Shop is a popular El Dorado National Forest granite crag at the base of the Lover's Leap formation on US-50, with accessible bouldering on the lower walls. Problems range V0–V5, on excellent featured Sierra granite. A shorter approach than the main Lover's Leap routes makes this a convenient session stop. Also a key transition wall for beginners moving from bouldering to sport climbing. Best season May–October. No permit required.

Pywiack Dome is a glacier-polished granite dome east of Tenaya Lake in Tuolumne Meadows with accessible low-angle slab bouldering at its base. Characteristic Tuolumne friction problems on exfoliated granite at 8,150 ft. Grades V0–V4 on clean, sun-warmed slabs. Often combined with Tenaya Lake swimming. NPS rock-climbing regulations apply; no permit needed for bouldering at base. Best season July–September. Part of the same high-altitude dome cluster as Lembert and Pothole Dome.
